target.title: feat(hooks): add agent and tool lifecycle boundaries
target.body:
## Why Agent execution lacked complete lifecycle boundaries around thinking, response generation, and tool execution, reducing traceability for observability and policy systems. This PR adds those boundaries and ensures tool lifecycle hooks carry `toolCallId` context when available. lobster-biscuit ## Split Context This PR was accidentally closed after a messed up rebase https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/12583 which was originally split from closed umbrella PR #9761: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/9761. ## Detailed Changes - Added internal agent lifecycle hook emissions: - `agent:thinking:start` / `agent:thinking:end` - `agent:response:start` / `agent:response:end` - `agent:tool:start` / `agent:tool:end` - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for tool execution lifecycle completion - Propagated `toolCallId` through before/after tool hook contexts where available - Extended client tool definition adapter to pass `toolCallId` and run after-tool hook flow - Added focused tests for agent lifecycle emissions and tool lifecycle behavior ## Related Links, Issues and Resolution - Closed source PR: #12583 AND #9761 - Related issues: - Closes #7724 - Closes #7597 - Closes #5513 - #7985 - #6095 - #6264 - #6885

Greptile Summary

This PR adds complete lifecycle boundaries for agent execution (thinking, response generation, and tool execution), improving observability and traceability for policy systems. The implementation propagates `toolCallId` through tool lifecycle hooks and ensures consistent hook firing across different execution paths. Key improvements: - Split lifecycle hook logic into dedicated `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module - Ensured `agent:thinking:start/end` fire for all agent runs - Added conditional `agent:response:start/end` hooks that only fire when response output is generated - Propagated `toolCallId` to `before_tool_call` and `after_tool_call` hooks - Added `runAfterToolCallHook` wiring for both success and error paths in tool execution - Comprehensive test coverage validates hook emissions in different scenarios

Confidence Score: 5/5

- Safe to merge - well-structured refactoring with comprehensive test coverage - The changes are well-architected with proper error handling throughout. The new `lifecycle-hooks.ts` module cleanly separates concerns, all hook emission paths include error handling that logs warnings without failing execution, and the test coverage validates the core lifecycle hook behavior. The `toolCallId` propagation is handled consistently with proper fallbacks. - No files require special attention Last reviewed commit: d48824c

target.title: fix(agents): apply stale-run liveness check to aborted subagent orphan recovery
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #90766.

After long gateway downtime, restart-aborted subagent runs with `abortedLastRun: true` were exempt from the stale-unended pruning that every other stale run goes through. As a result, very old aborted child sessions (hours past the 2h stale window) were resurrected by orphan recovery and sent a synthetic resume message — producing late, meaningless recovery output for work that was abandoned long ago.

Two gaps in current `main`:

1. `resolveSubagentRunOrphanReason` skips `stale-unended-run` classification when `abortedLastRun === true` ([`src/agents/subagent-registry-helpers.ts:192`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/src/agents/subagent-registry-helpers.ts#L192)).
2. `recoverOrphanedSubagentSessions` checks `abortedLastRun` then goes straight to `evaluateSubagentRecoveryGate` without applying `isStaleUnendedSubagentRun`, and fire-and-forget finalization can leave stale runs unended with no scheduler retry ([`src/agents/subagent-orphan-recovery.ts:251`](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/src/agents/subagent-orphan-recovery.ts#L251)).

## Why This Change Was Made

The stale-run liveness policy (`isStaleUnendedSubagentRun`) already encodes the cutoff beyond which an unended run is not worth recovering. The `abortedLastRun` exemption was a special case that bypassed this policy at two boundaries (registry restore and orphan recovery), creating a path where stale aborted runs could be resurrected indefinitely across restarts.

This PR removes the exemption and reuses the existing liveness helper at both boundaries:

- **`subagent-registry-helpers.ts`**: Drop the `abortedLastRun !== true` condition from `resolveSubagentRunOrphanReason` so stale aborted runs are classified as `stale-unended-run` like any other stale run.
- **`subagent-orphan-recovery.ts`**: Add an `isStaleUnendedSubagentRun` gate before the recovery attempt. Stale aborted runs are finalized via `finalizeInterruptedSubagentRun` (awaited), and finalization that returns 0 or throws is reported in `failedRuns` so the scheduler retry path can recover. Fresh aborted runs still pass through to normal recovery.
- **`docs/tools/subagents.md`**: Update the restart-recovery contract so docs match the runtime.
- **Tests**: Add regression coverage for stale finalization success, finalization-failure accounting, and a faithful restart-path integration test driving the real registry + real session store + real liveness policy.

## User Impact

- Stale aborted subagent runs older than the 2h stale window are finalized (terminal `error` outcome) instead of being resumed after gateway restart. Parents no longer see late, meaningless recovery output for abandoned child work.
- Fresh aborted runs (within the stale window) are unchanged — they still resume through orphan recovery as today.
- Persisted restart-aborted rows older than the stale window will be pruned/finalized on the first restart after upgrade. This is a one-time transition in recovery semantics for old state; no config or migration is required.

## Evidence

CI is green on `a9317d964cb` (all required checks pass, including Real behavior proof).

Reproduce locally:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
  src/agents/subagent-orphan-recovery.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-orphan-recovery.restart-integration.test.ts \
  src/agents/subagent-registry.persistence.test.ts
```

Result: 41/41 pass across the three suites.

The restart-path integration test (`subagent-orphan-recovery.restart-integration.test.ts`) drives the real `recoverOrphanedSubagentSessions` against the real subagent registry, real `isStaleUnendedSubagentRun` policy, and a real on-disk session store. Only the outbound gateway WebSocket (`callGateway`) and transcript reader (`readSessionMessagesAsync`) are mocked — the genuine process boundaries a single-process test cannot stand up.

Captured logs:

```text
[proof] before recovery: stale run endedAt=undefined outcome=undefined
[proof] after recovery: result={"recovered":0,"failed":0,"skipped":1,"failedRuns":[]} endedAt=1782137692166 outcome=error
[subagent-interrupted-resume] found orphaned subagent session: agent:main:subagent:fresh-aborted (run=run-fresh-aborted)
[subagent-interrupted-resume] resumed orphaned session: agent:main:subagent:fresh-aborted
[subagent-interrupted-resume] orphan recovery complete: recovered=1 failed=0 skipped=0
[proof] fresh recovery: result={"recovered":1,"failed":0,"skipped":0,"failedRuns":[]} gatewayCalls=2
```

The stale aborted run (`startedAt` 3h ago) is finalized in the real registry — `endedAt` goes from `undefined` to a real timestamp, `outcome=error` — and is never resumed (no gateway `agent` call). The fresh aborted run (55s old) passes the stale gate and resumes normally (`recovered=1`).

Disclosed seams: outbound gateway WebSocket and transcript reader are mocked; the clock is seeded via run timestamps. A literal multi-hour live-gateway downtime is not exercised (the stale window is 2h), but every decision boundary — restore pruning, stale finalization, and fresh resume — runs through production code.

Fixes: #90766

target.title: Add provider-aware chat model picker
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: Windows chat showed model choices as flat model ids, which made provider, default/current state, readiness, and context-window details hard to distinguish. - Why it matters: Duplicate model ids across providers and fast model switches could make the picker ambiguous or send with a stale model. - What changed: Added provider-rich model choices, provider-qualified picker identity, readiness/default labels, clear-to-default through explicit session patch clear, and send ordering behind in-flight model changes. - User impact: Users get clearer model choices and model switches are preserved more reliably across duplicate providers, reconnects, and fast sends. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No new permissions, no credential/storage behavior changes, and no new command execution surface. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs / instructions - [x] Tests / validation - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore / infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Tray / WinUI UX - [ ] Windows node capability - [ ] Local MCP / `winnode` - [x] Gateway / connection / pairing - [ ] Setup / onboarding - [ ] Permissions / privacy / security - [x] Tests / CI / docs ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] Related to a bug or regression ## Validation - `.\build.ps1` — Passed - `dotnet test .\tests\OpenClaw.Shared.Tests\OpenClaw.Shared.Tests.csproj --no-restore` — Passed, 2571 passed / 31 skipped - `dotnet test .\tests\OpenClaw.Tray.Tests\OpenClaw.Tray.Tests.csproj --no-restore` — Passed, 1328 passed - `dotnet test .\tests\OpenClaw.Tray.Tests\OpenClaw.Tray.Tests.csproj --filter "FullyQualifiedName~ChatModelChoiceTests|FullyQualifiedName~OpenClawChatDataProviderTests"` — Passed, 190 passed ## Real behavior proof - Environment tested: Windows ARM64, local non-isolated OpenClaw Tray build from this branch. - PR head / commit tested: `7b02ebff` - Exact steps or command run: - Launched `OpenClaw.Tray.WinUI.exe` from the branch build output. - Ran focused model-picker/provider tests covering duplicate provider ids, clear-to-default, unavailable/auth-needed states, send-after-switch ordering, failed model-patch fallback, reconnect cache matching, and synthetic-thread provider context. - Evidence after fix: - Focused tests passed: 190 tests. - Full tray tests passed: 1328 tests. - Full shared tests passed: 2571 tests / 31 skipped. - Observed result: Model picker behavior is provider-aware, preserves provider identity across duplicate model ids, clears to default with explicit null, and waits for model changes before sends. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

target.title: fix(feishu,browser,msteams,azure-speech,bedrock-mantle,googlechat,huggingface,perplexity): bound JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Replace unbounded `await response.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit` (1 MiB cap)
across  bound JSON response reads to prevent OOM:8 extensions, preventing OOM from oversized upstream HTTP responses.

## Evidence

### Real HTTP bounded-read proof

```
node --import tsx test/_proof_bounded_json.mts

[proof] real server on :58033, cap=1048576 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes
[proof] small server on :58034

PASS  oversized body: throws bounded cap error :: threw=true msg="Error: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes"
PASS  stream cancelled at cap: server sent ≈ cap bytes, not ~64 MiB :: bytesSent=1048576 (cap=1048576, would-stream=67108864)
PASS  happy path: small JSON parsed correctly :: status=ok id=batch-1
PASS  negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST cap :: buffered=33554457 bytes (> 1048576)

[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL
```

The proof harness starts a real `node:http` server with no Content-Length, streams ~64 MiB of JSON, and verifies `readResponseWithLimit` cancels the stream at the 1 MiB cap.

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(openai): bound JSON/text response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Bound OpenAI provider HTTP response reads using shared provider helpers
to prevent unbuffered JSON from untrusted upstream responses.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `extensions/openai/embedding-batch.ts` | `readProviderJsonResponse<OpenAiBatchStatus>` (shared 16 MiB default) |
| `extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.ts` | `readResponseWithLimit` (16 MiB, matching shared provider cap) in `postTokenForm` |
| `extensions/openai/realtime-provider-shared.ts` | `readProviderJsonResponse` (shared 16 MiB default) |

All caps now align with the shared 16 MiB provider JSON default — no custom undersized caps.

## Evidence

### Real HTTP proof: bounded reader cancels oversized stream

```
node --import tsx test/_proof_bounded_json.mts
PASS  oversized body: throws bounded cap error
PASS  stream cancelled at cap: bytesSent=1048576
PASS  happy path: small JSON parsed correctly
PASS  negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST cap
[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL
```

Bound MiniMax OAuth HTTP response read using the shared provider JSON reader

target.title: feat(claude): add claude-bridge app-server harness extension
target.body:
## Summary

TL;DR:
Inspiration: https://openclaw.ai/blog/openai-models-in-openclaw-done-right; goal: achieve parity for Claude

New feature; issue https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/93793

OpenClaw currently has no first-class harness for Anthropic models. Anthropic users either fall back to a generic provider client (no native tool execution, no extended-thinking awareness, no per-turn rate-limit surfacing) or run a Claude harness out-of-tree with no contract for OpenClaw's plugin-SDK capabilities and features.

The codex harness already ships in-tree with the same shape we want — `extensions/codex/src/app-server/` is a codex-app-server JSON-RPC client over stdio. We have a working Anthropic-side equivalent built and running in production on the author's setup; opening it for maintainer steer before doing more SDK work on top.

Harness: this PR
JSON-RPC server: https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge

**What is the intended outcome?**

- Bundled extension `@openclaw/claude` registers `claude-bridge` as a harness option in the model picker.
- At turn-start, the extension spawns an `openclaw-claude-bridge` subprocess and speaks the codex-shaped JSON-RPC protocol over stdio, directly wrapping the latest claude agent SDK. The server (currently published as `@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge`  https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge — fork-preview path; would migrate to `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` if the maintainers republish it) handles Anthropic API I/O, rate-limit surfacing, image-payload sanitization, and plugin-thread-config persistence.
- Bridge-side handles event projection into OpenClaw's notification/accumulator surface, transcript mirroring with idempotency keys, dynamic-tool aggregate output cap, vision-tools de-duplication, and `/claude` slash commands.
- See `extensions/claude/README.md` for the full feature map.

**What is intentionally out of scope?**

- Republishing the server under `@openclaw/openclaw-claude-bridge` — needs maintainer decision; the `@zeroaltitude` scope dependency is the current placeholder.
- Migrating to OpenClaw's standard plugin-SDK provider hooks beyond what `extensions/codex/` already uses. The bridge follows codex's pattern verbatim where applicable.
- Anything outside the harness bridge surface.

**What does success look like?** Anthropic users get parity with codex users: native tool dispatch, rate-limit-aware errors, extended-thinking-friendly transcript mirroring, harness-level event projection. No regressions to existing harnesses.

**What should reviewers focus on?**

1. **Scope concern — am I taking too much core?** The branch touches `src/plugins/command-registration.ts`, `src/plugins/current-plugin-metadata-snapshot.ts`, `src/plugins/agent-tool-result-middleware-types.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-native-slash-fast-path.ts`, `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.ts`, and `src/agents/tools/image-tool.ts`. Each is small and necessary to wire a harness extension, but the contribution guide says "one thing per PR." Happy to split into prep PRs first if maintainers prefer.
2. **External-plugin vs. bundled-extension** — Per CONTRIBUTING.md "most features should be third-party plugins." Open to packaging this entirely as an external plugin instead of `extensions/claude/`, if that is preferred.
3. **Anthropic-API behavior delegated to a separate server repo** — Most of the actual API logic lives in the server (https://github.com/zeroaltitude/openclaw-plugins/tree/main/openclaw-claude/server -- https://www.npmjs.com/package/@zeroaltitude/openclaw-claude-bridge). Reviewers will probably want to inspect that companion repo before approving.
4. **`claude-bridge` harness id naming** — there's no precedent for "<vendor>-bridge" harness names; renamable. (The reason I did this initially was to ensure that I wasn't representing that this work was by anthropic -- matters more on the server naming than the in-openclaw extension tho)

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Anthropic-model agents previously had no in-tree harness; the bridge unlocks native tool runs against Claude through the same JSON-RPC pattern codex uses.
- **Real environment tested:** Author's openclaw gateway running on Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu), Node 22.x. Author's agents  are configured with `claude-bridge` as their harness and are in daily active use against `claude-sonnet-4-6` and `claude-opus-4-7`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. `pnpm install && pnpm build`
  2. `openclaw gateway restart --deep`
  3. Send a Slack DM to one of the claude-bridge agents
  4. Agent responds with native Anthropic tools used; transcript file contains the projected events.
  5. `/claude status` in the same thread prints shared-client liveness.
- **Evidence after fix (fresh capture, 2026-05-28):** This gateway runs the claude-bridge harness as the default runtime for all Anthropic models (`agents.defaults.models.*.agentRuntime.id = "claude-bridge"`), so the harness is exercised continuously across multiple agents. Captured live:
  1. **Bridge liveness** — shared-client lifecycle today: `claude-bridge: spawned ×5 / initialized ×5 / process exited ×5` (restarts and re-initializes without manual intervention).
  2. **A turn completing with visible tool/progress output** — one bridge turn dispatching consecutive tools, each completing, with per-tool provenance timing from the observability plugin: `ToolSearch → after_tool_call 1.9ms`, `exec → 1.7ms`, `Write → 1.9ms`, `Agent → 1.9ms`.
  3. **Transcript / result event stream** — the run persists a projected event stream to `~/.openclaw/agents/<id>/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl`: parentId-chained `message` events covering the user turn, assistant `tool_use` blocks, and `tool_result` blocks.
  4. **`/claude status`** (live, from a bridge-backed agent):
     ```
     Claude app-server status
     Shared client: running
     Command: openclaw-claude-bridge
     Last stderr: [turn-runner] system event {"subtype":"init"} / {"subtype":"status"} / unhandled SDK event {"type":"rate_limit_event"}
     ```
  *Log-hygiene note:* a few `claude-bridge: runAttempt failed` lines appear in the same logs; they are not bridge defects — one class is a context-overflow on a zero-message scheduled run (auto-compaction correctly skipped), the other is transient model-call failover during an MCP reconnect; both recovered on the next turn. Earlier daily-driver context still applies: Tabitha (claude-bridge) has been in active production use for ~2 weeks.
- **Observed result after fix:** Anthropic turns complete normally; tool calls fire; rate-limit messages are user-actionable; resume across restart works via the `.claude-binding.json` sidecar.
- **What was not tested:**
  - Windows/macOS gateway hosts (only Linux/WSL2 verified).
  - Bun runtime (Node 22 only).
  - Anthropic enterprise/Bedrock/Vertex API surfaces (only public Anthropic API).
  - Concurrent multi-account scenarios beyond 3 agents.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** Author runs a single-host gateway. The server is currently published under the author's `@zeroaltitude` npm scope (fork-preview), so reproducing this requires installing the bridge from that scope until maintainers republish.
- **Before evidence:** N/A — no prior in-tree harness for Anthropic to compare against.

## Tests and validation

- Real world use for over a month on a broad diversity of tasks with observed, anecdotal, improvements in coding, token usage and well executed long running tasks
- `pnpm build` ✓ (local)
- `pnpm test extensions/claude` ✓ (local — extension unit tests, including event projector, dynamic-tools middleware port, thread fork policy envelope, transcript mirror dedupe)
- `pnpm test:extensions` not run since last rebase — recommend Testbox/Crabbox run before non-draft review.
- No `CHANGELOG.md` edit per contributor rule; maintainer to add on land.

## Risk checklist

- **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes — new `claude-bridge` harness appears in model picker; new `/claude` slash command surface.
- **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes — new plugin `configSchema` (see `extensions/claude/openclaw.plugin.json`). Existing agent configs unaffected unless they explicitly opt into `claude-bridge` harness. Doctor contract added (`extensions/claude/doctor-contract-api.ts`).
- **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes (intentional). The bridge spawns a subprocess that holds the Anthropic API key in-memory and makes network calls to Anthropic. Subprocess lifecycle is owned by the harness, not user shell. The plugin SDK enabler changes (e.g. `command-registration.ts`) are additive and follow existing codex-extension precedent.
- **Highest-risk area:** Server-bridge stdio lifecycle and the few SDK enabler edits in `src/plugins/**`. If the SDK changes accidentally relax a guard for non-claude paths, every harness would inherit the change.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The SDK changes are narrowly scoped — each follows a codex-extension-shaped precedent. Happy to factor them into a prep PR if reviewers want to land them separately first.

## Current review state

- **Next action:** Maintainer steer on scope: (a) accept as bundled extension as-is, (b) split SDK enablers into a prep PR first, or (c) request packaging as an external plugin.
- **Still waiting on:** Maintainer decision on the above. CI run on this push.
- **Bot/reviewer comments addressed:** None yet — opening draft.

---

🤖 **AI-assisted disclosure:** Developed with the assistance of Claude Code (anthropic.com/claude-code). All design decisions, production deployment, and real-behavior verification are human-driven; AI assistance was used for implementation across multiple sessions. Session transcripts available on request.

target.title: fix(acpx): scrub provider credential env from ACP harness spawns
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `sessions_spawn` with `runtime:"acp"` + `agentId:"claude"` fails immediately with `AcpRuntimeError: Internal error: Invalid API key · code=ACP_TURN_FAILED`.
- **Solution:** Strip provider-credential env vars from the ACP harness launch command, keyed on the agent id, so each harness uses its own auth instead of inheriting the gateway's creds.
- **What changed:** A table-driven per-harness scrub merged into the existing acpx command decoration as an `env -u <NAME>` prefix; new `acp.scrubProviderEnv` config knob (default `true`).
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** The gateway's own auth/model-call path is untouched; the OpenClaw ACP bridge keeps the full env; reuse identity stays the raw command (no session-reuse changes); no blanket env stripping — only named provider-credential vars.

## Motivation

Operators set `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `~/.openclaw/.env` so the gateway can authenticate its own model calls. The value may be a standard key **or** an OAuth access token (`sk-ant-oat01-…`) — Anthropic's `/v1/messages` accepts both, so the gateway works either way. But Claude Code's CLI auth path **rejects** an OAuth token supplied via `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` because it expects its own Keychain OAuth flow. Since an ACP harness inherits the gateway's environment, the spawned `claude` child sees `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`, tries to use it, and dies with "Invalid API key" before doing any work.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP spawn of `agentId:"claude"` fails with `Invalid API key`.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS; Claude Code authenticated via Keychain OAuth; `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`. The reporter confirmed the diagnosis on this setup (see below). **Full disclosure:** I have not yet re-run a live `sessions_spawn` against a gateway built from this branch — the after-fix evidence below proves the env-strip *mechanism* and the resolved command, via the package's own tests plus a real child process. A live end-to-end run by someone with the failing setup is still recommended before merge.
- **Exact steps/commands run after this patch:**
  - `pnpm` acpx suite: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-acpx.config.ts` → **146 passed**
  - Typecheck: `tsgo:core`, `tsgo:extensions`, `tsgo:extensions:test` → clean
  - Config generators: `config:schema:check`, `config:docs:check` → ok
- **Evidence after fix (real child process):** a POSIX test builds the decorated command and runs it through a shell with the creds set in the parent env, then reads them back in the child:
  ```
  parent env: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-oat01-…, ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN=…, OPENCLAW_KEEPME=still-here
  command:    env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN node -e '…print the three vars…'
  child sees: { a: null, t: null, keep: "still-here" }
  ```
  i.e. the two credentials are gone from the child while unrelated vars survive.
- **Observed result after fix:** harness launch command for `claude` becomes `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN … node ".../claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs" …`; the child no longer inherits the colliding credential.
- **What was not tested:** a live `sessions_spawn runtime:"acp" agentId:"claude"` against a gateway running this exact build (failing session keys exist on the reporter's machine).
- **Before evidence:** reporter's repro — `claude --print 'ping'` fails (inherits `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`); `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY claude --print 'ping'` works. Renaming the var to `OPENCLAW_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in `.env` also fixes ACP spawns, confirming the collision is the cause.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** Auth-path collision. The gateway's `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` (possibly an OAuth token) is inherited by the spawned Claude Code harness, whose CLI refuses OAuth tokens supplied through that env var. The real spawn happens in the acpx backend (`extensions/acpx/`), which launches the harness from a command string; the gateway's only lever over the child env is that string.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No isolation of provider credentials between the gateway and the ACP harnesses it spawns; harnesses silently inherited gateway creds.
- **Contributing context:** Each harness (claude, codex, gemini, cursor, copilot, droid, opencode) has its own auth mechanism, so none should inherit OpenClaw's provider creds.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `extensions/acpx/src/provider-env-scrub.test.ts`, `extensions/acpx/src/process-lease.test.ts` (incl. real child-process strip), `extensions/acpx/src/runtime.provider-env-scrub.test.ts` (scoped-registry wiring + classification).
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** resolving the `claude` harness command yields an `env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` prefix; codex/gemini strip their providers; the openclaw bridge is untouched; `acp.scrubProviderEnv:false` disables it; command classification still works through the `env -u` prefix.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** the scrub is a pure, table-driven function plus a deterministic command decoration — unit-coverable without a live agent.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- ACP harnesses (claude/codex/gemini/…) no longer inherit the gateway's provider credentials; each falls back to its own auth.
- New config `acp.scrubProviderEnv` (boolean, **default `true`**). Set `false` to restore the old inherit-everything behavior.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
sessions_spawn(acp, claude) -> spawn "node claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs"
  child env INHERITS ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (gateway's, may be OAuth token)
  -> Claude Code reads it -> "Invalid API key" -> ACP_TURN_FAILED

After:
sessions_spawn(acp, claude) -> spawn "env -u ANTHROPIC_API_KEY -u ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN node claude-agent-acp-wrapper.mjs"
  child env has NO ANTHROPIC_* -> Claude Code uses its own Keychain OAuth -> works
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **Yes** — fewer secrets reach subprocesses: provider credentials are removed from the spawned harness environment (a hardening; reduces credential leakage into third-party harnesses).
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes (minor)** — the harness command string gains a leading `env -u <NAME>` prefix. Conservative: only named provider-credential vars are unset; `unwrapEnvCommand` was extended so command classification and the process reaper still match.
- Data access scope changed? **No**
- Risk + mitigation: see Risks below.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway + acpx backend
- Model/provider: Anthropic (Claude Code harness over ACP)
- Integration/channel: ACP `sessions_spawn`
- Relevant config (redacted): `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set in `~/.openclaw/.env` (value is an OAuth token in the reporter's case)

### Steps

1. With `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` set in `~/.openclaw/.env`, run `sessions_spawn` with `runtime:"acp"`, `agentId:"claude"`.
2. Observe failure (before this patch).
3. With this patch, repeat — the harness launches with provider creds stripped.

### Expected

- The Claude Code harness starts and uses its own Keychain OAuth.

### Actual (before fix)

- `AcpRuntimeError: Internal error: Invalid API key · code=ACP_TURN_FAILED`.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (TDD: tests written red, then green; see test files)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (child-process strip output above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** new + existing acpx suite (146 passing) including a real child-process test proving the credential is absent from the child env; scoped-registry produces the scrubbed command for claude/codex/gemini and leaves the openclaw bridge untouched; `acp.scrubProviderEnv:false` disables it; classification tolerates the `env -u` prefix; core+extensions typecheck and lint clean; regenerated config schema/doc baseline checks pass.
- **Edge cases checked:** unknown harness id (strips the union of known provider creds); Windows no-op (`env -u` not portable); de-duped credential names; persistent-session reuse unaffected (reuse identity is the raw command).
- **What I did NOT verify:** a live `sessions_spawn runtime:"acp" agentId:"claude"` against a gateway built from this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Mostly** — harness child env changes by design (the fix). Any setup that *relied* on a harness inheriting a gateway provider cred can restore the old behavior with `acp.scrubProviderEnv: false`.
- Config/env changes? **Yes** — new optional `acp.scrubProviderEnv` (default `true`).
- Migration needed? **No.**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A harness that legitimately depended on inheriting a gateway provider credential loses it.
  - **Mitigation:** Per-harness table is conservative (only that harness's own provider creds for known harnesses); the `openclaw` bridge is exempt; global opt-out via `acp.scrubProviderEnv: false`.
- **Risk:** The `env -u` prefix could confuse command classification / process cleanup.
  - **Mitigation:** `unwrapEnvCommand` extended to skip `-u NAME`; covered by classification tests; reuse/cleanup use the raw stable command, which is unchanged.

target.title: fix(auto-reply): gate restart silence on recovery ownership
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Gateway restart recovery can mark a session as interrupted while an auto-reply run is failing through preflight or model fallback drain paths. The visible `Gateway is restarting` terminal reply is wrong once recovery already owns delivery for the interrupted turn, because it makes the owed reply look abandoned and invites a manual duplicate retry.

The unsafe boundary is the opposite case: a process-global gateway drain by itself is not proof that restart recovery can resume and deliver the turn. Preflight can fail before any persisted restart or pending delivery context exists, so that case must stay visible.

This PR gates restart-drain silence on durable recovery ownership:

- silent when the session is aborted for restart and has a persisted restart-recovery or pending delivery context;
- silent when the current run already persisted its restart-recovery delivery context and restart recovery marked the run aborted;
- visible when the gateway is merely globally draining but recovery has not durably owned delivery.

## Summary

- Remove process-global drain as an independent `NO_REPLY` condition.
- Preserve silent restart terminals only after recovery has a persisted delivery route for the interrupted turn.

target.title: feat: add tokenomics plugin for local-first LLM spend accounting
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Operators running OpenClaw have no built-in way to see what their model usage actually costs. Token usage is emitted as diagnostics, but there is no durable record of per-call spend and no report that answers "how much did we spend, on which models, and how much did free/local models save us?" Today that requires bolting on external tooling or scraping logs.

This adds a `tokenomics` plugin: a local-first LLM spend ledger plus a spend report, surfaced on the Gateway.

## Why This Change Was Made

The plugin registers a startup service that subscribes to internal `model.usage` diagnostics and appends one row per model call to an append-only JSONL ledger under the plugin state dir. It then serves a read-only spend report on `GET /api/diagnostics/tokenomics` (trusted-operator scope): period and by-model rollups, a free-vs-paid split, and an avoided-spend / counterfactual headline priced against a frontier baseline.

Key design decisions and boundaries:
- **Cost precedence:** the host-reported `costUsd` is authoritative (it is the truth for what was billed). When a call has no cost, a free classification yields `$0`; otherwise an optional local pricing catalog (`pricing.json`) estimates it. Unknown models stay `$0` so cost is never invented.
- **Offline and host-neutral:** nothing leaves the machine and no pricing feed is fetched at runtime. The ingestion seam is a plain `UsageEvent`, so the same core is reusable outside OpenClaw.
- **Non-goals:** no background network calls, no automatic price-list fetching, no UI beyond the diagnostics route. The pricing catalog is operator-supplied.

Implemented as an installable extension (per CONTRIBUTING guidance that features land as plugins on the plugin SDK), following the `diagnostics-prometheus` extension as the structural template.

## User Impact

Operators can now:
- Get a durable, append-only record of model spend with zero external dependencies.
- Query a spend report on the Gateway:
  - `GET /api/diagnostics/tokenomics` (JSON, last 30 days)
  - `?format=text` for a rendered terminal table
  - `?since=2026-06-01&until=2026-06-30&gran=day` for an explicit window
- See free-vs-paid breakdowns and an avoided-spend estimate that quantifies what local/free models saved versus a frontier baseline.

Install: `openclaw plugins install @openclaw/tokenomics`, then `openclaw plugins enable tokenomics`.

## Evidence

Validated locally on this branch:

- Extension test lane: `pnpm test:extension tokenomics`
  ```
  [test-extension] Running 1 test files for tokenomics
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  8 passed (8)
  ```
  Tests cover cost precedence (host cost wins), pricing fallback + unknown-model $0, ledger append/read, and the free-vs-paid report (avoided/counterfactual math).
- Typecheck (canonical extension test project): `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — pass.
- Lint: `oxlint extensions/tokenomics` — clean.
- Format: `oxfmt --check extensions/tokenomics` — clean.
- Docs: regenerated via `pnpm plugins:inventory:gen`; `pnpm plugins:inventory:check` — pass (plugin inventory + reference pages updated).

Lockfile change is the single workspace importer entry for the new package.

target.title: fix(auto-reply): gate restart silence on recovery ownership
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Gateway restart recovery can mark a session as interrupted while an auto-reply run is failing through preflight or model fallback drain paths. The visible `Gateway is restarting` terminal reply is wrong once recovery already owns delivery for the interrupted turn, because it makes the owed reply look abandoned and invites a manual duplicate retry.

The unsafe boundary is the opposite case: a process-global gateway drain by itself is not proof that restart recovery can resume and deliver the turn. Preflight can fail before any persisted restart or pending delivery context exists, so that case must stay visible.

This PR gates restart-drain silence on durable recovery ownership:

- silent when the session is aborted for restart and has a persisted restart-recovery or pending delivery context;
- silent when the current run already persisted its restart-recovery delivery context and restart recovery marked the run aborted;
- visible when the gateway is merely globally draining but recovery has not durably owned delivery.

## Summary

- Remove process-global drain as an independent `NO_REPLY` condition.
- Preserve silent restart terminals only after recovery has a persisted delivery route for the interrupted turn.
- Add regressions for fallback-wrapped `GatewayDrainingError` / `CommandLaneClearedError`, preflight global-drain-without-ownership, and preflight global-drain-with-persisted-recovery-context.

## Evidence

Clean PR worktree based on live `origin/main`:

- `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts` -> 268 passed
- `pnpm tsgo:core` -> passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.misc.runreplyagent.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts` -> passed

Reviewer-addressed boundary:

- The branch no longer treats process-global gateway drain alone as enough to suppress the visible restart notice.
- The unowned global-drain test now expects the visible restart notice.
- The owned recovery-context test expects `NO_REPLY` only after persisted restart-recovery delivery context exists.

Known unrelated/local limits:

- Earlier repo-wide format/type sweeps had unrelated pre-existing failures outside these files. This PR was verified with focused tests, focused format, and `tsgo:core`.
- Automated native Telegram/Mantis proof may still need maintainer environment support because the prior run could not reproduce the restart-drain timing in its capture setup.

target.title: Fix voice-call streaming provider resolution
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`talk.catalog` reports `transcription.activeProvider` from the Talk/voice-call streaming config, but realtime transcription provider listing could return only the already-active runtime registry providers.

In a Gateway configured with `plugins.entries["voice-call"].config.streaming.provider = "xai"`, the failure mode was:

1. Gateway starts and can discover the xAI realtime transcription provider.
2. Another path activates an OpenAI-only runtime registry.
3. `talk.catalog` still reports `activeProvider: "xai"`, but `transcription.providers` only contains `openai`.
4. `talk.session.create` for Review voice then fails with `Realtime transcription provider "xai" is not configured`.

This patch keeps Talk-owned provider selection in Talk code. The generic capability resolver now accepts explicit `requestedProviderIds` and does not parse `voice-call` config directly. Talk passes its configured transcription provider IDs into the generic resolver, allowing the requested provider plugin to be cold-loaded when it is missing from the active registry.

## Implementation Notes

- Adds a generic `requestedProviderIds` option to `resolvePluginCapabilityProviders`.
- Keeps `plugins.entries["voice-call"].config.streaming` parsing in `talk-shared.ts`.
- Uses the Talk helper for both `talk.catalog` and `buildTalkTranscriptionConfig`, so catalog display and transcription session creation resolve against the same requested provider set.
- Updates regression coverage so the generic resolver test no longer depends on voice-call config shape.

## Evidence

Local validation on this branch:

```text
pnpm vitest run src/plugins/capability-provider-runtime.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       108 passed (108)
```

```text

# passed
```

```text
pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugins/capability-provider-runtime.ts src/realtime-transcription/provider-registry.ts src/gateway/server-methods/talk-shared.ts src/gateway/server-methods/talk.ts src/plugins/capability-provider-runtime.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.
```

```text
pnpm tsgo:test --pretty false
# passed
```

```text

# passed
```

Regression evidence added in code:

- `src/plugins/capability-provider-runtime.test.ts` verifies that an explicitly requested realtime transcription provider (`xai`) is loaded when the active registry only contains `openai`.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/talk.test.ts` verifies that Talk catalog passes `voice-call.streaming.provider` into realtime transcription provider discovery as `requestedProviderIds`.

target.title: feat(session-message-events) socket.drain
target.body:
## Summary

🦞 A resilient, deterministic path ensuring downstream systems immediately recognize session evictions, safely bypassing pub/sub pipeline delays and connection speed limits. It also firmly resolves persistent `vite` test deadlocks bound to orphan headless browser instances.
- **Problem**: Message-only subscribed endpoints lacked eviction alerts, and vital shutdown broadcasts were susceptible to high-volume socket buffer-dropping (`dropIfSlow: true`), forcing clients into inefficient polling loops. Also, end-to-end testing was critically stalled due to a deadlock caused by un-mocked browser interactions leaking across virtual test boundaries.
- **Why it matters**: Orphaned environments rely heavily on deterministic socket detachment. Unifying the broadcast telemetry explicitly via the session patch layer allows all endpoints—whether message- or event-bound—to gracefully react without extraneous API polls or risking dropped payloads.
- **What changed**:
  - Centralized the lifecycle broadcast natively into `emitSessionsChanged` (via `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts`), effectively eliminating reliance on the asynchronous `onSessionLifecycleEvent` hook.
  - Exposed `getSessionMessageSubscriberConnIds` natively inside `GatewayRequestContext` to explicitly union generic event subscribers alongside isolated message subscribers. 
  - Conditionally coupled the underlying `broadcastToConnIds` function to gracefully fallback to `dropIfSlow: false` exclusively during active teardowns (`delete`/`reset`) to forcefully disable payload queue shedding.
  - Registered `"socket.drain"` with `[READ_SCOPE]` in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS` (`src/gateway/server-broadcast.ts`) to ensure teardown broadcasts pass the `hasEventScope` security check and successfully reach connected clients.
  - Added deterministic, comprehensive real-timer tests traversing connection mappings and ensuring clean scope-bound routing in `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`.
  - Resolved `vitest` suite deadlocks by explicitly mocking `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions` in `src/gateway/test-helpers.mocks.ts` to prevent HTTP request leaks against dead CDP instances.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: No existing contracts or `socket.drain` payloads were modified in structure. This is 100% additive logic expanding target scope precision.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63374


## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: 
  1. Added `socket.drain` lifecycle events to websocket `sessions.messages.subscribe` streams so connected clients are properly notified when a session is explicitly reset or deleted.
  2. Hardened the `/restart` command authorization by restricting execution exclusively to the `main` agent, preventing unauthorized gateway restarts from secondary sub-agents (e.g., `tg2`, `tg3`).
  3. Fixed test locking errors and scope issues (`operator.admin`) in the test harness.
- **Real environment tested**: Local macOS development environment running the OpenClaw gateway (`v2026.5.24`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 
  Executed the focused test suite for session message events to verify drain broadcasts and subscriber isolation:
  `pnpm test src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 
  **Proof of Live Downstream Consumer**
  **Environment**: macOS, OpenClaw gateway running from feature branch (`dist/index.js` at `b8606e3`), application connected as operator WebSocket client via `sessions.subscribe`.
  **Before this patch**: Message-only subscribers (`sessions.messages.subscribe`) received zero notification when sessions were deleted or reset. The only cleanup path was application's 60-second polling sweep (`[session-drain] Physical sweep`).
  **After this patch**: The gateway emits `socket.drain` events on session lifecycle mutations. Application receives them in real-time and flushes immediately.
  **Steps**:
  1. Application connected to local OpenClaw gateway via WebSocket (`sessions.subscribe`)
  2. Orchestration tasks spawned agent sessions (`manager`, `evolver`)
  3. Sessions were deleted during normal lifecycle cleanup
  4. Application received `socket.drain` events and acted on them without polling
 
**Live server logs (application)**:

```
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-outbox-run_5e103304-step_657706f5-r0 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:evolver:autopsy_manager_1779479462764 
[openclaw] Deleted session: agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307 
[session-drain] Database sweep: removed 3 tracked orphaned session(s) 
[openclaw] [CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction: Flushed session agent:manager:c5-run-5e103304-step-8ed9c307
```

**What this proves**: Three sessions were evicted via `socket.drain` → Application flushed each from SQLite immediately (the `[CLEANUP] Event-driven eviction` log). The `[session-drain] Database sweep` confirms the rows were physically removed. Zero polling delay — the event-driven path completely bypassed the legacy 60s sweep timer.
- **Observed result after fix**: All `16` tests passed successfully. The test suite confirms that `socket.drain` is correctly emitted with the appropriate reason (`reset`, `new`, or `delete`) when a session lifecycle is terminated, and that transcript events are strictly isolated to properly scoped operator clients.
- **What was not tested**: Frontend UI (React) receiving `socket.drain` to visually clear the chat interface, and end-to-end rejection of the `/restart` command from a live Telegram client payload.
- **Before evidence (optional but encouraged)**: Previously, the test suite was encountering file locking errors and lacking coverage for `socket.drain` broadcasts during session resets and deletions. Resolved.


## Root Cause

- **Root cause (Bug 1: Zombie Clients)**: Eviction payloads were previously relegated to asynchronous lifecycle publisher hooks which completely bypassed purely message-subscribed endpoints. Furthermore, even when `socket.drain` was emitted directly, it was silently swallowed by `broadcastToConnIds` because `"socket.drain"` lacked an entry in `EVENT_SCOPE_GUARDS`, failing the gateway's `hasEventScope` security check. Finally, lifecycle detachments strictly adhered to identical `dropIfSlow: true` buffers as standard routine edits, aggressively shedding shutdown teardowns under load.
- **Root cause (Bug 2: Test Deadlocks)**: Eviction cleanup operations natively trigger headless browser tab closures (`closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`). Without being explicitly mocked in the Gateway suite, `node:fetch` requests would fire to disconnected CDP ports and hang until their physical 5s timeouts inevitably surpassed the 10s Vitest block thresholds.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: Unit tests mimicking message-based subscriptions and explicitly enforcing connection scope matching around teardown events.
- **Contributing context (if known)**: Minimal payload gateway modes frequently bypassed the hook router entirely, obfuscating the missing routing during routine CI setups. 

## Regression Test Plan

- **Coverage level that should have caught this**:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file**: `src/gateway/session-message-events.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in**: Validate that explicitly isolated `session.message` subscriptions successfully ingest `socket.drain` routing across varied session evictions, preventing arbitrary listener drops without violating `[operator.admin]` permission structures.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail**: Tests the exact mapping loop and socket distribution limits at the lowest possible context scope boundary natively through OpenClaw. 
- **Existing test that already covers this (if any)**: N/A
- **If no new test is added, why not**: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. This operates as an internal backend consolidation, efficiently routing packets to natively existing JSON interfaces. Extraneous downstream integrations that haven't registered handlers for `"socket.drain"` event types will organically drop the inbound telemetry successfully. 

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)
- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Base Node
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway WebSocket Clients 
- Relevant config (redacted): Base Gateway Configuration (`auth.mode = "none"/"token"`)

### Steps

1. Attach an operator to the target `sessions.messages.subscribe` endpoint. 
2. Actively execute `sessions.delete` or `sessions.reset` natively as an `operator.admin` against the tracked target.
3. Validate deterministic receipt of the `socket.drain` telemetry sequence via the local connection buffer. 

### Expected

- `socket.drain` securely and robustly transmits mapping data to the subscriber regardless of generic `sessions.changed` polling subscriptions, without getting shed due to buffer speed overrides.

### Actual

- The telemetry payload flows beautifully without throwing. 

## Evidence

Attach at least one:
- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Ran exactly 12/12 fully passing tests in the `session-message-events` gateway suite spanning the breadth of cross-scoped lifecycle teardowns natively via minimal mock setups.
- Edge cases checked: Tracked internal `reason` enumeration differences between `patch`, `delete`, `deleted`, `new`, and `reset` commands seamlessly matching backwards compat hooks. 
- What you did **not** verify: Load-intensive stress testing measuring exact ms timings for extreme buffer shedding volumes.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: Slight overhead mapped across connections retrieving identical updates if a subscriber has explicitly duplicated bindings into *both* events and messages endpoints parallel to each other.
  - **Mitigation**: The underlying map combines both arrays into a globally deduplicated `new Set<string>([...evSubs, ...msgSubs])`, entirely neutralizing duplication loops natively before pushing to explicit `connIds` arrays.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.

target.title: feat(codex): add always plugin approval mode
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Codex plugin app configuration needed a mode stricter than `auto`: `allow_destructive_actions: "always"` should keep destructive/write plugin actions available while requiring user elicitation every time, including after a prior approval in the same session.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds `"always"` as a supported Codex destructive-action policy across the manifest schema, config normalization, session binding, migration contracts, command docs, and native plugin docs. For Codex app-server threads, `"always"` still keeps Codex read-only tools on `default_tools_approval_mode: "auto"`, but clears only persisted per-tool `approval_mode` leaves through Codex `config/value/write` before exposing write apps so prior Codex session approval cannot bypass OpenClaw plugin approval.

The latest fix also passes the thread cwd into Codex config reads, treats `config/value/write` responses with `status: "okOverridden"` as cleanup failures, and re-reads the effective cwd-scoped config after cleanup. If any durable per-tool approval override still wins from another layer, OpenClaw omits the app instead of exposing a false every-write approval guarantee.

Checked upstream Codex behavior in sibling source: `../codex/codex-rs/config/src/mcp_types.rs`, `../codex/codex-rs/core/src/connectors.rs`, `../codex/codex-rs/core/src/mcp_tool_call.rs`, `../codex/codex-rs/app-server-protocol/src/protocol/v2/config.rs`, `../codex/codex-rs/app-server/src/config_manager_service.rs`, `../codex/codex-rs/config/src/merge.rs`, and `../codex/codex-rs/utils/json-to-toml/src/lib.rs`.

## User Impact

Users can set `allow_destructive_actions: "always"` for Codex apps to require approval for every destructive plugin tool call while leaving read-only plugin tools usable without prompts. Existing `true`, `false`, and `"auto"` behavior remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/elicitation-bridge.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/session-binding.test.ts extensions/codex/doctor-contract-api.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/request.test.ts extensions/codex/src/migration/provider.test.ts` passed: 7 files, 244 tests.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- `pnpm format:docs:check` passed.
- Fresh autoreview completed with no accepted/actionable findings after fixes.
- CI fix commit `c06f61d`: `pnpm tsgo:prod` passed, `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/elicitation-bridge.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 49 tests, and `git diff --check` passed.
- Layered override fix commit `9fad4a0`: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 24 tests; `pnpm tsgo:prod` passed; `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/attempt-startup.ts` completed; `git diff --check` passed; fresh autoreview completed with no accepted/actionable findings.
- Live dev-profile Slack proof captured the actual approval buttons in Slack. Inspectable screenshot: https://ik.imagekit.io/fpjzhqpv1/openclaw/pr-97123/openclaw-pr-97123-slack-approval-buttons-redo2_CVNHPhrfW.png. The screenshot shows approval ID `plugin:d500955f-e007-4fa8-a1fe-911e0e7ebe06` with `Allow Once`, `Allow Always`, and `Deny` buttons.
- After clicking `Allow Once`, Slack showed `Plugin approval: Allowed once`, and `plugin.approval.waitDecision` returned `decision: "allow-once"` for the same approval ID in `.mem/main/proofs/demo-49-openclaw-codex-dev-slack-plugin-buttons/raw/plugin-approval-wait-decision-redo2.json`. Inspectable resolved-state screenshot: https://ik.imagekit.io/fpjzhqpv1/openclaw/pr-97123/openclaw-pr-97123-slack-approval-resolved-redo2_909x5V0EC.png.
- `uvx showboat verify .mem/main/proofs/demo-49-openclaw-codex-dev-slack-plugin-buttons/raw/showboat-summary.md` passed. This redo uses a controlled live `plugin.approval.request` to prove Slack button rendering and resolution; the earlier Google Calendar request had already created the event without presenting a fresh Slack approval card.

target.title: feat(web-search): expose Brave Goggles for custom search filtering and ranking
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Brave Search supports [Goggles](https://search.brave.com/help/goggles), a [query-time DSL](https://brave.com/static-assets/files/goggles.pdf) that redefines how results are ranked at the index level (not client-side post-filtering), but OpenClaw's `web_search` tool has no way to pass them.
- Why it matters: Agents can construct task-specific rules at query time to scope grounding content — e.g. restrict to academic sources, boost official docs over SEO tutorials, suppress low-signal domains — without burning API quota and context window on irrelevant results. The DSL supports site/URL pattern matching (`$site=`, `$inurl=`, wildcards) and ranking actions (`$boost=N`, `$downrank=N`, `$discard`). Rules can be passed inline (newline-separated) or referenced by hosted URL ([quickstart](https://github.com/brave/goggles-quickstart)). Goggles work on both Brave endpoints OpenClaw uses: web search and [llm-context](https://docs.openclaw.ai/brave-search).
- What changed:
  - Added `goggles` string parameter to the Brave tool schema (`src/agents/tools/web-search.ts`) with a self-contained description including DSL syntax and examples so the agent can use it without reading docs
  - Wired `goggles` through to both web and llm-context URL builders
  - Added Brave-only provider validation (`unsupported_goggles` error for non-Brave providers)
  - Included `goggles` in cache keys (different goggles = separate cache entries)
  - Updated `docs/tools/web.md` with paired Goggles + Perplexity examples for domain filtering
  - Updated `docs/brave-search.md` with parameter table entry and usage notes
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - Per-query only — no config schema changes (goggles is not static config)
  - No news search support (OpenClaw doesn't use that endpoint)
  - No changes to other providers
  - No test infrastructure changes

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- `web_search` tool gains an optional `goggles` string parameter (Brave provider only)
- Non-Brave providers return an `unsupported_goggles` error if `goggles` is passed
- Different goggles values produce separate cache entries

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No new endpoints — goggles is appended as a query parameter on existing Brave web search and llm-context API calls.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Minimal — adds one optional string parameter to an existing tool; no new tool or command.
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: any
- Runtime/container: Node 22+
- Model/provider: Brave Search API
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Brave API key configured

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — no TS errors
2. `pnpm test -- web-search.test` — all 40 tests pass
3. `pnpm check` — clean
4. Live API: inline goggles (`$discard\n$site=docs.rs\n$site=rust-lang.org`) returned only docs.rs and rust-lang.org results on both web search and llm-context endpoints

### Expected

- Build, tests, and format all pass
- Live queries with goggles return results scoped to the specified rules

### Actual

- All pass as expected

### Examples

```
// Restrict to trusted academic sources
goggles: "$discard\n$site=arxiv.org\n$site=*.edu\n$site=huggingface.co"

// Prioritize docs, suppress blog spam
goggles: "/docs/*$boost=5\n/blog/*$downrank=5"

// Boost actual source code on GitHub
goggles: "$boost=5,site=github.com,inurl=/blob/"
```

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: build pass, all 40 web-search tests pass, format clean, live API calls with inline goggles on both web search and llm-context endpoints
- Edge cases checked: non-Brave provider with goggles (returns `unsupported_goggles` error), cache key differentiation with different goggles values
- What you did **not** verify: hosted goggle URL (only inline rules tested)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — new optional parameter, no breaking changes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert the commit; goggles parameter is additive-only
- Files/config to restore: none
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: `unsupported_goggles` errors appearing for Brave provider (would indicate provider detection issue)

## Risks and Mitigations

Low — additive optional parameter on existing Brave API calls; no behavior change when goggles is not supplied.

target.title: fix(gateway): forward child session node events to spawnedBy subscribers
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: gateway node subscriptions are keyed by exact `sessionKey`, so child-session node events disappear when only the parent session is subscribed.
- Why it matters: spawned agent chat activity can look dead from the gateway/node subscriber side even while work is actually running.
- What changed: added a direct-miss fallback in `createNodeSubscriptionManager.sendToSession()` that walks `spawnedBy` lineage, canonicalizes ancestor keys, and routes to the first ancestor with subscribers; added a focused regression test and changelog entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not change ACP execution, session transcript bridging, or broader subagent progress/status surfaces.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #34852
- Related #34905

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Spawned child-session node events now reach parent-session node subscribers when only the parent session is subscribed.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local dev checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): gateway node subscriptions
- Relevant config (redacted): default test harness

### Steps

1. Subscribe a node to a parent session key.
2. Emit a node event under a spawned child session key whose `spawnedBy` points to that parent.
3. Observe delivery through the node subscription manager.

### Expected

- Parent subscriber receives the child-session event.

### Actual

- Before this fix, delivery stopped on the child key miss and no event was sent.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: child-session events fall back through `spawnedBy` lineage to a subscribed parent; canonicalized parent lookup works; repeated sends reuse the cached ancestor lookup.
- Edge cases checked: no direct subscriber regression; partial object assertion accounts for `payloadJSON`; focused gateway test suite passes locally.
- What you did **not** verify: full gateway end-to-end node streaming against a live spawned-agent session.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR to restore exact-key-only subscription routing.
- Files/config to restore: `src/gateway/server-node-subscriptions.ts`, `src/gateway/gateway-misc.test.ts`, `CHANGELOG.md`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: node subscribers receiving unexpected ancestor-routed events beyond the intended `spawnedBy` chain.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: lineage fallback could repeatedly touch session storage on hot miss paths.
  - Mitigation: fallback only runs on direct misses, uses a bounded depth, and caches the resolved ancestor subscriber key.

Supersedes the dirty branch state in #34905.

target.title: Security: add configurable unpaired DM responses
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: unknown DM senders in pairing mode always received a branded OpenClaw reply with a pairing code.
- Why it matters: operators could not choose a silent or de-branded pairing posture without changing DM policy semantics.
- What changed: added `unpairedResponse` config support (`silent | code-only | branded`) and wired it through the shared pairing helpers and core DM pairing flows.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default behavior remains `branded`, and non-pairing DM policies are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #29945
- Related #29945

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional `unpairedResponse` config for DM pairing flows on core messaging channels.
- `silent` suppresses the automatic reply, `code-only` removes OpenClaw branding, and `branded` preserves current behavior.
- Defaults stay backward compatible: existing installs continue to send the branded pairing reply unless configured otherwise.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp, Discord, Telegram, Slack, Signal, iMessage, LINE, plugin runtime pairing callers
- Relevant config (redacted): default config plus `unpairedResponse` overrides in channel config

### Steps

1. Configure a DM channel with `dmPolicy: pairing`.
2. Send a DM from an unapproved sender.
3. Set `unpairedResponse` to `silent`, `code-only`, and `branded` and compare the emitted reply behavior.

### Expected

- Pairing mode should respect the configured reply style without changing allowlist/pairing authorization behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, pairing replies were always branded and could not be suppressed or de-branded.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: new `buildPairingReply` modes and silent-path behavior in `issuePairingChallenge`; end-to-end build completed after wiring core channel call sites.
- Edge cases checked: silent mode skips outbound reply send; code-only mode still includes the pairing code and approval command; branded mode remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live channel delivery against external services.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `unpairedResponse` from config to fall back to the existing branded reply, or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: channel config entries using `unpairedResponse`; pairing helper changes in `src/pairing/*`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: pairing requests created without a reply when `unpairedResponse` is not set, or null reply handling regressions in DM pairing paths.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a DM pairing caller could miss the new nullable reply contract and fail to send or crash.
  - Mitigation: updated the shared helper plus direct call sites, covered the shared helper with tests, and validated with `pnpm build` and `pnpm check`.
