target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve text-block boundaries in draft preview (#87322)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87322 by giving Mattermost the same text-block boundary handling Discord has had for years. Today the draft preview edits a single post across every phase of a tool-heavy turn, so earlier text blocks disappear before the user reads them - Issue 1 in the report. The reporter's explicit `streaming: "off"` + `blockStreaming: true` config also failed because the inbound path forced `disableBlockStreaming: true` unconditionally - Issue 2 in the report.

This PR keeps the existing canonical `streaming.mode: "off" | "partial" | "block" | "progress"` contract on main (`src/config/types.base.ts:12`) - no schema change, no new config keys.

## Behavior change

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` - inbound dispatch path (`monitorMattermostProvider`):

- **Block-reply path now honored when preview is off.** Replaced the hardcoded `disableBlockStreaming: true` with the same conditional already used by the button-click (`monitor.ts:832`) and slash-command (`monitor.ts:1047`) paths: when `draftPreviewEnabled` is true the preview is still the visible sink (no change), but when `streaming.mode === "off"` the path now honors the operator's explicit `account.blockStreaming` setting so block replies create separate posts via `createMattermostPost`.
- **Preview rotates at text-block boundaries when `streaming.mode === "block"`.** New `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` in `draft-stream.ts` holds a "did we just stream something?" gate. At `onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, and `onToolStart` the controller calls `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` (the primitive already on the stream - `draft-stream.ts:133`) if and only if a draft update was emitted since the last boundary. The next `draftStream.update(...)` then runs the `else` arm of `sendOrEditStreamMessage` (`draft-stream.ts:92-104`) and `POST /posts` creates a new Mattermost post; earlier sealed posts stay visible. The gate is the same shape Discord uses (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`) - back-to-back boundaries without content never emit empty preview posts.
- **All other modes unchanged.** `streaming.mode === "partial"` (default) keeps the edit-in-place feel. `"progress"` keeps its single rolling progress post. `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts` - added `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController({ enabled, forceNewMessage })`. Local helper, no exposure through `api.ts`.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` - added unit tests for the controller (enabled/disabled, content gate, back-to-back boundaries) and for `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` itself (next update creates a new `POST /posts` call).

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` - extended the existing `vi.mock("./draft-stream.js", …)` shim with a no-op `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` stub so the suite keeps wiring through the mocked draft module.

## Compatibility and exported surface (maintainer sign-off requested)

Two intentional changes need explicit maintainer acceptance before merge:

- **Visible delivery-shape change.** For `streaming.mode: "block"` and for preview-off `blockStreaming: true`, a tool-heavy turn now produces multiple visible Mattermost posts (one per text/tool boundary) instead of one repeatedly-edited post. This is the fix for #87322 (earlier blocks were being overwritten), but it is a user-visible change for operators already running those configurations. Default `streaming.mode: "partial"` and `"progress"` are unchanged, and `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.
- **Exported draft-loop surface.** This adds an optional `takePending?(): string` member to the exported `DraftStreamLoop` type (`src/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound`). It is additive and optional, so existing plugin-SDK implementors are not broken, but it is a plugin-SDK-adjacent surface change that maintainers should accept.

I cannot accept these on the maintainers' behalf; flagging them here so the delivery-shape and exported-type changes are an explicit pre-merge decision.

## Diff size

```
 docs/channels/mattermost.md                        |   6 +-
 docs/concepts/progress-drafts.md                   |  16 +-
 docs/concepts/streaming.md                         |   5 +-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts | 271 ++++++++++++++++++++-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts      |  76 ++++++-
 .../monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts           | 111 ++++++++++-
 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts    |  34 ++-
 src/channels/draft-stream-loop.ts                  |  10 +
 8 files changed, 502 insertions(+), 27 deletions(-)
```

## Boundary flush (addresses ClawSweeper P1 finding)

ClawSweeper's first review flagged a race: `noteBoundary()` calling `forceNewMessage()` synchronously could clear `streamPostId` while a draft update's `POST /posts` was still in flight; the in-flight response would then set `streamPostId` after the boundary, and the next block would edit the previous post instead of creating a new one.

`forceNewMessage` is now async and awaits `loop.flush()` before clearing `streamPostId` and resetting pending state. The controller's `noteBoundary` is async too; the inbound-dispatch callbacks (`onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, `onToolStart`) await it before resetting `lastPartialText`. A new `draft-stream.test.ts` regression case (`flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post`) holds the first `POST /posts` response promise, fires the boundary while the create is still in flight, then asserts the second block lands as a separate `POST /posts` (not a `PUT /posts/{id}` edit of the first post). A second new case (`awaits the forceNewMessage promise before resolving the boundary`) verifies the boundary promise stays pending until `forceNewMessage` resolves.

## Pending-partial boundary seal (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:167-168`)

ClawSweeper's latest re-review flagged a deeper boundary race. When a block's first `POST /posts` create is still in flight and a later cumulative `onPartialReply` leaves text pending for the same block, `forceNewMessage` bumped `postGeneration` and cleared `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. When the create then resolved, its `gen === postGeneration` guard failed because the generation had already advanced, so the returned post id was discarded. `sealedPostId` had already been captured as `undefined`, so the pending text went out as a second `POST /posts` instead of editing the just-created post. A slow server left the user with both a stale partial post and the sealed block post.

Fix (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts`): `forceNewMessage` takes the pending seal text first (cancelling the throttle timer), then synchronously bumps `postGeneration` and clears the live `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. Each create now records its post id keyed by the generation it was issued under (`createdPostIdsByGeneration`), so after the await the boundary recovers the finished block's real post id from that record and edits it with the pending cumulative text instead of creating a duplicate. Clearing the cursor before the await (rather than after) is what also makes the detach synchronous; see the next section for why that ordering is load-bearing under fire-and-forget callbacks.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it`): queues a second cumulative partial while the first create is held in flight, then fires the boundary and asserts the request sequence is `POST /posts` then `PUT /posts/post-1` (one create, one in-place edit). The previous ordering produced `['/posts', '/posts']` (two creates, the duplicate ClawSweeper flagged); the fix produces `['/posts', '/posts/post-1']`. The generation-keyed record is what lets the seal still find the just-created post id even though the generation is bumped before the await.

Block-mode tool-progress test correction (`monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`): the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives three `bash` progress events with distinct commands and `detailMode: "raw"`. Bare same-tool progress with hidden detail renders to an identical preview line, and the progress compositor correctly dedups identical text to a single update, so the prior version asserted three preview updates against one rendered line. With distinct rendered lines the case now exercises its real intent: three in-place updates on one post, no boundary for same-tool progress, and exactly one boundary when a new tool starts.

## Synchronous boundary detach (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:165-168`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review made the load-bearing point that the embedded-agent subscriber does not await the boundary callback. `onPartialReply`/`onReasoningStream` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts:281,1193`) and `onAssistantMessageStart`/`onReasoningEnd` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts:144,573,687`) are all dispatched with `void`. So the boundary callback's async tail is not sequenced before the next partial. With the previous ordering, `forceNewMessage` awaited the in-flight op *before* clearing `streamPostId`, which meant the live post id stayed attached to the finished block across the await. A partial for the next block that arrived during that await - flushing immediately once the throttle window was open - would read the still-attached `streamPostId` and `PUT` its text onto the previous block's post, overwriting it. Whether the overwrite happened depended on microtask ordering between the boundary's clear and the partial's flush, which is exactly the kind of "incorrect under the real caller" the finding called out.

Fix: `forceNewMessage` now bumps the generation and clears `streamPostId` synchronously, before it awaits anything. The instant the boundary fires, the live cursor is detached, so any fire-and-forget partial that arrives next deterministically opens a fresh post regardless of microtask ordering. The seal onto the previous block's in-flight create is preserved via the generation-keyed record described above. This is the "detach synchronously" option from the review.

Real behavior proof (live HTTP, fire-and-forget interleave): a loopback Mattermost v4 server (`POST /api/v4/posts` artificially held 120ms) driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` over real `fetch` round-trips. Block #1 creates `post-1`; the throttle window is allowed to elapse; the boundary is then fired fire-and-forget (`void forceNewMessage()`, not awaited, as the embedded subscriber does), and block #2's partial arrives next. The same script was run against the committed pre-fix source (`56186cfe25`) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `56186cfe25`):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = "post-1"
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-1  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 1  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
=> block #1 post was OVERWRITTEN by block #2 (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = undefined
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Looking into the logs now..."
=> block #1 preserved on its own post; block #2 landed on a separate post (NO overwrite).
```

The one observable that flips the outcome is `stream.postId()` immediately after the fire-and-forget boundary call: `"post-1"` (still attached, before fix) versus `undefined` (detached synchronously, after fix). With the cursor detached the next partial creates `post-2`; without it, it `PUT`s onto `post-1` and the first block's text is gone.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles`): fires `forceNewMessage()` without awaiting it, asserts `postId()` is already `undefined`, then updates with the next block's text and asserts two `POST /posts` creates (`post-1`, `post-2`) rather than an edit of `post-1`. It fails on the committed source (`expected 'post-1' to be undefined`) and passes on the fix.

## Boundary gate clobber (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:78`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review found the matching race one level up, in the gate. `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` left `hasStreamedContent` true while it awaited `forceNewMessage()`, then reset it to false after the await. Both `noteBoundary()` and `noteUpdate()` are dispatched fire-and-forget by the embedded subscriber, so the next block can call `noteUpdate()` (setting the flag true) while a prior boundary is still awaiting. When that older boundary resumes it sets the flag back to false, swallowing the streamed-content signal, so the following boundary sees `hasStreamedContent === false` and skips `forceNewMessage()`. The block after it then edits into the previous post instead of getting its own.

Fix: `noteBoundary()` now returns early when nothing was streamed, then clears `hasStreamedContent` synchronously before awaiting `forceNewMessage()`. A `noteUpdate()` that arrives during the await sets the flag true again and is preserved for the next boundary, so no boundary is skipped.

Real behavior proof through the actual embedded callback path (current head): rather than calling the controller directly, this proof drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts`) and lets it dispatch `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onPartialReply` with its real `void` fire-and-forget semantics. Those callbacks are wired exactly as the Mattermost monitor wires them (`onPartialReply -> updateDraftFromPartial -> draftStream.update + noteUpdate`; `onAssistantMessageStart -> await noteBoundary`) against the production `createMattermostDraftStream` + `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` over the loopback Mattermost v4 server. The script emits a real event stream: assistant message #1 + text delta (block A), then message #2 start (boundary 1) immediately followed in the same synchronous event turn by block B's text delta (so the partial lands while boundary 1 is still awaiting the in-flight `POST`), then message #3 start (boundary 2) + block C. Run against the committed head (`c8c90e0df8`, gate unfixed) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `c8c90e0df8`, buggy gate):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-2  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 2
=> only 2 posts: a boundary was skipped and a block was overwritten (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-3  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 3  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block B: found the bad commit"
   post-3 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 3
=> all three blocks preserved on separate posts (real embedded callback ordering).
```

Same production subscriber, same callback wiring, same loopback server on both passes; only the `draft-stream.ts` source differs. Before the fix the swallowed `noteUpdate()` makes boundary 2 skip rotation, so block C `PUT`s onto block B's `post-2` and block B's text is lost (2 posts). After the fix boundary 2 rotates and block C lands on `post-3` (3 posts). This is the current head exercised through the genuine embedded `void` callback ordering, not a direct controller call.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending`): holds `forceNewMessage()` in flight for the first boundary, calls `noteUpdate()` during that await, releases, then fires a second boundary and asserts `forceNewMessage` is called twice. On the buggy gate the clobber leaves the flag false and the second call never happens (1 call).

Environment and reproduction for the fire-and-forget proofs above: Node 22.19 on Linux, throwaway scripts (not committed) that import the production modules and drive them over a loopback Mattermost v4 HTTP server on `127.0.0.1` (random port, `POST /api/v4/posts` held 120ms to simulate latency); the client issues real `fetch` round-trips. The gate-clobber proof additionally drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` and lets it dispatch the callbacks with its real `void` semantics (no direct controller calls). No real tokens, channels, or non-loopback endpoints are used (bearer is a placeholder string, host is `127.0.0.1`). Each before/after pair runs the same script against the committed pre-fix source and the patched source: the synchronous-detach pair compares `56186cfe25` (pre-detach) vs the detach fix; the gate-clobber pair compares `c8c90e0df8` (detach fix present, gate unfixed) vs this head.

## Monitor callback local-state ordering (addresses ClawSweeper P1 at `monitor.ts:1950`)

The same fire-and-forget anti-pattern existed one more level up, in the monitor's boundary callbacks. `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onReasoningEnd` awaited `previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` and only afterward reset the per-turn local state (`lastPartialText = ""`, `progressDraft.reset()`). Because the embedded subscriber dispatches these callbacks with `void`, the next block's `onPartialReply` can run during that await, while `lastPartialText` still holds the previous block's text. `updateDraftFromPartial` treats a shorter prefix of the current draft as a stale repaint and drops it, so a new block whose first partial is a prefix of the prior block can be discarded entirely.

Fix (`monitor.ts`): both callbacks now start the split, reset the local state synchronously, then await: `const boundarySettled = previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary(); lastPartialText = ""; progressDraft.reset(); ... await boundarySettled;`. The reset is sequenced with the boundary start rather than deferred past the await.

Real behavior proof through the embedded path (same `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` harness, current head, only the `onAssistantMessageStart` ordering toggled to mirror the monitor before/after): block A streams a long line whose `POST` is still in flight; the boundary fires; block B's only partial is `"Reverting"`, a prefix of block A's `"Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"`, and arrives during the boundary await.

```
===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = OLD (await, then reset) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   distinct posts: 1
   => block B was DROPPED: its prefix partial was deduped against stale block-A text during the boundary await.

===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = NEW (start split, reset synchronously, then await) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   post-2 message="Reverting"
   distinct posts: 2
   => block B preserved on its own post.
```

A committed regression for this lives at the monitor closure level, which is only reachable by driving `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (core `src/agents`) together with the real stream; importing core internals from an extension test would break the plugin boundary (`extensions/AGENTS.md`), so this is proven via the embedded-path capture above rather than a colocated unit test.

## Real behavior proof (feature-level: block rotation)

This earlier capture demonstrates the core block-rotation feature (preview-disabled vs `streaming.mode: "block"`) and remains valid; the embedded/fire-and-forget callback race is covered by the two sections immediately above, which were captured on the current head.

Behavior addressed: Mattermost draft preview overwrites earlier text blocks within a single turn, and `streaming: "off" + blockStreaming: true` does not produce separate posts (#87322). User-visible symptom is message-loss across tool-heavy turns.

Real environment tested: Node 26.0.0 on macOS 26.0 (Darwin 25.3.0), local Mattermost-API-shape loopback HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:38765` with an artificial 120ms `POST /api/v4/posts` response delay to simulate live Mattermost server latency. Exercised the production `createMattermostClient` (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts:93`) - including its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` adapter with `allowPrivateNetwork: true` - driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` (`draft-stream.ts:56`) and the new `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController`. The loopback server speaks the live Mattermost v4 API surface: `POST /api/v4/posts` returns a `{id, create_at, message}` body for the create path after the 120ms hold; `PUT /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns the patched post for the edit path; `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns `{status: "OK"}`. Bot token, channel id, and thread root id are real-shape values. The AFTER pass deliberately fires the next boundary while the previous block's `POST` response is still in flight (`fastBoundary: true`) so the run exercises the race ClawSweeper flagged. The fix branch is at commit 353f83b794.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
git checkout fix/87322-mattermost-block-preview-boundaries
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node --import tsx proof-87322.mjs
```

The script (`proof-87322.mjs`, not committed) starts the loopback server, imports the production source modules (no test mocks), drives the same callback sequence the inbound dispatcher fires for a tool-heavy turn - `onPartialReply` text block, `onToolStart` boundary, `onAssistantMessageStart` boundary, repeated - first with the boundary controller `enabled: false` (current shipped behavior), then with `enabled: true` (the new `streaming.mode: "block"` wiring), reusing the same client + channel + thread on both passes.

Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the loopback server with the 120ms POST delay and the fast-boundary scenario, showing the actual HTTP method, path, message body, assigned post id, and the controller awaiting the in-flight create before forcing a new message - 
```
[23:57:48.660] Fake Mattermost server listening on http://127.0.0.1:38765 (POST artificially delayed 120ms to simulate live latency)
[23:57:48.661]
===== BEFORE FIX (streaming.mode='partial' or boundary disabled) (boundary controller enabled=false, fastBoundary=false) =====
[23:57:48.661]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.661] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.731] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-1 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:48.861] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.862] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:48.863] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.864] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:48.865] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.865] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:48.866] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.867] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."
[23:57:48.918]
===== AFTER FIX (streaming.mode='block', boundary controller enabled, FAST boundary with in-flight POST) (boundary controller enabled=true, fastBoundary=true) =====
[23:57:48.918]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY - fast, POST still in flight]
[23:57:48.918]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:48.919] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-2 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.041] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.042] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-3 message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.164] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.166] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-4 message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.290] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.292] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-5 message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:49.415]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.417] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-6 message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."

===== SUMMARY =====
[23:57:49.540] BEFORE: 1 POST /posts (creates), 4 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] AFTER : 5 POST /posts (creates), 0 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] => AFTER produces 5 distinct Mattermost posts; BEFORE collapses to a single post with 4 in-place edits, which is the issue #87322 user-visible symptom.
```

Key timing detail in the AFTER pass: every boundary in the fast scenario fires _before_ the previous block's `POST` response comes back (e.g. `23:57:48.918` boundary fires while `post-2` is still being held by the server), and the controller's `forceNewMessage()` line shows it correctly awaiting the in-flight create - `awaiting pending flush` at t=48.918, then `resolved` at t=49.041, ~120ms later, exactly the server's hold time. The next block's `POST` only goes out after the resolved line. No `PUT` (in-place edit) is ever emitted, so the race ClawSweeper flagged at `draft-stream.ts:74-75` cannot collapse the next block onto the previous post id.

Observed result after fix: with the boundary controller wired in (the AFTER block above), the production `MattermostClient` issues `POST /api/v4/posts` for every text/tool block boundary - five distinct Mattermost posts (`post-2` through `post-6`), each carrying one block of the turn's content. The BEFORE block shows the shipped behavior on `main`: one `POST /api/v4/posts` for the first block (`post-1`), then four `PUT /api/v4/posts/post-1` calls that overwrite the visible message at every transition. Only the last `PUT` body (`"Done - revert is in. Watching CI."`) is what a Mattermost user actually sees - earlier text blocks and tool calls are gone. The patched path preserves them as their own posts. Same `MattermostClient` build, same channel, same thread, same callback sequence - only the controller's `enabled` flag differs. The terminal capture above is the exact stdout from the loopback HTTP server logging every inbound request as it lands.

Supplemental unit/integration coverage (not the primary proof; these only assert the same boundary the live capture shows above):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` → 20/20 passed (includes the `forceNewMessage` regression, the `flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post` delayed-transport regression, the `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it` regression, the `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles` synchronous-detach regression, the new `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending` gate-clobber regression, and 5 controller cases).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` → 10/10 passed (after extending the existing draft-stream module mock with a no-op controller stub; the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives distinct rendered progress lines).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` → 29 files / 377/377 passed (full mattermost dir suite, includes `monitor.test.ts` and `accounts.test.ts`).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` / `pnpm tsgo:extensions` / `pnpm check:test-types` → all exit 0.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` and `oxfmt --check` on touched files → exit 0.
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata` → exit 0 (canonical `streaming.mode` values already shipped; no schema change in this PR).

What was not tested: A self-hosted Mattermost 11.6.2 Team Edition Docker server with a real OpenClaw bot account and a real provider running an exec-heavy agent was not exercised end-to-end here - that environment was not reachable from this checkout. The capture above instead drives the same production `createMattermostClient` (including its SSRF-guarded fetch adapter) against a v4-API-shape loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, so the HTTP-level behavior the live Mattermost server would see is identical, but the Mattermost server-side rendering pipeline (notification fan-out, mobile push, etc.) was not observed. The reporter on #87322 can confirm the user-visible result against their Mattermost 11.6.2 server by following the same `streaming.mode: "block"` config on the fix branch.

## Refs

- Fixes #87322
- Related (not closed): #75256 - overlapping branch attempted a narrower `streaming.mode = "partial" | "block"` config that CLAWsweeper flagged as schema-narrowing. This PR keeps the canonical `streaming.mode` contract intact and only attaches per-mode behavior to the existing `"block"` value.
- Mirrors the Discord boundary pattern at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`.

cc the reporter on #87322 for the live Mattermost server-side verification path.

PR surface: Source +13, Tests +61. Total +74 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. for source-level reproduction: current main and v2026.6.10 still call `.trim()` directly on awaited setup prompt text in the token replacement and generic text-input paths. I did not run the full interactive macOS onboarding sequence in this read-only review.

target.title: fix(channels): defensively handle non-string prompter.text() results in token replace flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Stop calling `.trim()` directly on `await prompter.text({...})` in `promptSingleChannelToken` (`src/channels/plugins/setup-wizard-helpers.ts`) and in the `runTextInputSteps` block inside `runChannelSetupWizard` (`src/channels/plugins/setup-wizard.ts`). Replace both call sites with `normalizeOptionalString(raw) ?? ""`, which trims when the prompter returns a string and falls back to `""` for any non-string value — eliminating the `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` crash reported in #67366 during the `openclaw onboard` Telegram bot-token replace flow.
- Treat the empty-string fallback as "no token entered" — the existing downstream `if (plainResult.token) { … } else return { action: "keep" }` path in `promptSingleChannelSecretInput` naturally degrades to the "keep current value" outcome, so the user is no longer thrown into a crash mid-onboarding and can retry instead of having `openclaw onboard` exit with a stack trace.
- Add 3 colocated unit cases in `setup-wizard-helpers.test.ts` driving `promptSingleChannelToken` directly: the production-shaped "replace configured Telegram token" path with `prompter.text` returning `undefined`, the env-decline path with the same `undefined` return, and a positive regression test that whitespace around a real bot token is still trimmed cleanly.

## Behavior change to flag

`openclaw onboard` (and `openclaw channels add` plus any other flow that goes through `runChannelSetupWizard`) no longer crashes with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` when the Clack text prompt returns a non-string value during the Telegram bot-token replace branch (issue body's steps 1–8). The flow now treats that case as "no replacement token entered" and falls through to the existing keep-current-config path, allowing the user to retry instead of losing the entire onboarding session. Sessions where the user enters a valid token are unaffected — the trim still runs through `normalizeOptionalString`, which is the same trim+empty-check helper already used across the surrounding wizard validators.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `WizardPrompter.text` is typed as `Promise<string>`, but the production crash report and community confirmations on #67366 (multiple users on `2026.4.x` / `2026.4.14`) show the runtime can resolve the awaited value to `undefined` during the token-replace branch. Both unsafe call sites that chained `.trim()` directly onto the awaited prompter result now go through `normalizeOptionalString(raw) ?? ""`, which is the existing shared helper that returns the trimmed string for strings and `undefined` for non-strings.
- **Real environment tested:** local macOS arm64 source checkout post-rebase against `upstream/main`; behavior verified through 3 new colocated vitest cases in `src/channels/plugins/setup-wizard-helpers.test.ts` driving `promptSingleChannelToken` with a stub prompter that explicitly resolves `text` to `undefined` (the exact runtime shape that produced the crash) and the existing 89-case suite for the same file kept passing unchanged.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  - `pnpm test src/channels/plugins/setup-wizard-helpers.test.ts`
  - `pnpm test:changed`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - `prompter.text → undefined` + `(accountConfigured: true, hasConfigToken: true)` (replace-existing-token path) → `promptSingleChannelToken` resolves to `{ useEnv: false, token: "" }` instead of throwing. The previous `(await …).trim()` line would now have been `(undefined).trim()` — the exact #67366 crash.
  - `prompter.text → undefined` + `(canUseEnv: true)` after the user declines env → also resolves to `{ useEnv: false, token: "" }` (the second crash-prone branch is also defended).
  - Whitespace handling preserved: `prompter.text → "  1234567890:ABCdefGHI  "` resolves to `{ useEnv: false, token: "1234567890:ABCdefGHI" }`. The trim semantics that downstream consumers rely on are unchanged for real string inputs.
- **Observed result after fix:**
  - `src/channels/plugins/setup-wizard-helpers.test.ts`: 92 / 92 passed (89 existing + 3 new) in ~810 ms.
  - `pnpm test:changed`: 7,330+ tests passed across the affected lanes. The only red is the pre-existing `src/tui/tui-pty-local.e2e.test.ts > drives the real local backend with a mocked model endpoint` flake (the PTY child exits with `Node.js v22.19+ is required (current: v22.14.0)` on the local checkout), which reproduces on pristine `upstream/main` and has no overlap with `src/channels/**` or any wizard prompt path.
  - `pnpm check:changed`: typecheck core + extensions + tests, lint, import cycles, plugin-sdk wildcard / dependency-pin / package-patch guards all green. The only red is the pre-existing `npm shrinkwrap guard (31 packages)` failure that reproduces on pristine `upstream/main`.
- **What was not tested:** the exact reproduction sequence from #67366 on a live macOS shell (`openclaw onboard` → QuickStart → telegram → Modify settings → Enter Telegram bot token → "Keep it?" No → Enter a new Telegram bot token → confirm no crash). The defensive guard at the call sites cannot reproduce the unknown root cause that lets Clack's `text()` resolve to a non-string value, but it does verifiably eliminate the symptom — the `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` is no longer reachable from either of the two known `.trim()` call sites on the wizard prompt path. The community comments under the issue (multiple users on `2026.4.14`, `2026.4.x`, and 2026.5.x) describing exactly this crash were the upstream evidence used to identify the symptom site.

## Notes

- This is a **symptom-targeted defensive fix**, not a root-cause fix for "why does `WizardPrompter.text` resolve to `undefined` in this branch". The type contract says it cannot, but the production crash trace says it does, and chasing the underlying Clack / runtime mismatch is out of scope for this PR. The wizard layer's existing `normalizeOptionalString` helper is the same `string | undefined` adapter used in the validator immediately above, so going through it is consistent with the surrounding code rather than a one-off `?? ""` paste.
- The unrelated #67693 ("Prevent setup prompts from crashing on blank option labels") changes `clack-prompter.ts`'s `select`/`multiselect` option-label normalization. It does **not** touch the `text()` return path, so it does not cover #67366 — verified by inspecting that PR's diff.
- Both call sites that pull text from the prompter and immediately `.trim()` are updated in the same change; no other unsafe `.trim()` on a prompter-text result remains in `src/channels/plugins/**`.
- Reviewed against root `AGENTS.md`, `src/channels/CLAUDE.md`, and `CONTRIBUTING.md`. No `CHANGELOG.md` edits (per CONTRIBUTING).

Refs #67366

target.title: Skip QMD memory search when scope denies
target.body:
## Summary

- short-circuit QMD-backed `memory_search` when the current session is denied by `memory.qmd.scope`
- return an empty successful result with `debug.skipped = "skipped_by_scope"` instead of opening the memory manager
- keep the QMD manager's own scope guard as defense in depth, but log it as `skipped_by_scope` at debug level instead of a warning-style denial

## Why

Denied QMD memory searches are correctly blocked today, but the request can still reach the QMD manager in channel/thread contexts that are outside scope. This creates noisy `qmd search denied by scope` journal entries and can waste work before the denial is known.

## Validation

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-tool-manager-mock.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts -- -t "logs when qmd scope denies search"`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

Note: running the full `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts` file on current `origin/main` still shows unrelated pre-existing warning-mock accumulation failures in other tests; the changed scope-denial test passes when targeted.

target.title: docs(backup): describe manual restore flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Document that `openclaw backup` currently supports create/verify, not a first-class restore command.
- Add manual restore guidance for trusted archives: verify before extraction, stage into a private temporary directory with cleanup, inspect `manifest.json`, use manifest asset paths rather than filename-derived roots, restore selected payloads, then run doctor after restart.
- Add archive layout, same-machine/new-machine restore guidance, plugin dependency follow-up, and a migration-doc link while keeping runtime behavior unchanged.
- Clarify that `openclaw backup verify` checks archive structure/payload layout only; it does not authenticate an archive or make untrusted content safe.

Fixes #77849

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: The backup docs listed create and verify flows but did not tell operators how to restore from a backup archive, and the CLI does not currently expose `openclaw backup restore`.
- **Real environment tested**: Repository checkout `/root/projects/openclaw-backup-restore-guidance`, PR head `8895257160`, changed file `docs/cli/backup.md`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Inspected the backup docs and source references; reviewed the PR diff with `git diff origin/main...HEAD -- docs/cli/backup.md`; ran `git diff --check`; ran `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/cli/backup.md`; ran `pnpm docs:check-links`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Copied live terminal output from the patched checkout:

```text
docs/cli/backup.md:24:OpenClaw does not currently provide an `openclaw backup restore` command. Restore is a manual copy-back flow guided by the embedded `manifest.json`. See [Restore from an archive](#restore-from-an-archive).
docs/cli/backup.md:84:## Restore from an archive
docs/cli/backup.md:86:OpenClaw does not currently provide an `openclaw backup restore` command. Archives are plain `.tar.gz` files, and the embedded `manifest.json` records the source paths and archive paths needed for a manual restore.
docs/cli/backup.md:100:trap 'rm -rf "$restore_dir"' EXIT
docs/cli/backup.md:110:The manifest reports `archiveRoot`, `paths.stateDir`, `paths.configPath`, `paths.oauthDir`, `paths.workspaceDirs`, and an `assets[]` list. Each asset includes its `kind`, original `sourcePath`, and `archivePath` inside the tarball. Use those manifest fields as the source of truth before copying anything back.
docs/cli/backup.md:112:Do not derive the archive root from the `.tar.gz` filename. `openclaw backup create --output` can write to a custom filename while the embedded `archiveRoot` remains timestamp-derived.
docs/cli/backup.md:114:### Archive layout
docs/cli/backup.md:127:### Restore selected paths
docs/cli/backup.md:180:- [Migrating an OpenClaw install](/install/migrating)
Docs MDX check passed (1 files, 49ms).
$ node scripts/docs-link-audit.mjs
checked_internal_links=4480
broken_links=0
```

`git diff --check` completed with no output.
- **Observed result after fix**: The public backup CLI docs now have a manual restore guide for the current archive format, with structural archive verification before extraction, private staging with cleanup for sensitive archive contents, manifest-driven path selection, archive-layout explanation, same-machine/new-machine guidance, post-restore doctor/plugin follow-up, and explicit separation between verification and archive trust/authenticity.
- **What was not tested**: No live restore was performed because this is documentation for the current manual restore procedure, not a new restore implementation.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/cli/backup.md`
- `pnpm docs:check-links`

target.title: i18n(zh-CN): Fix and unify Chinese translation
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem**: The zh-CN translation file had 3 issues: (1) new panel fields were untranslated (English fallback), (2) manually-reviewed translations from the previous PR were overwritten by the CI locale sync script, (3) terminology was inconsistent — / were translated differently across contexts, and some strings had obvious machine-translation quality. `activity` `delivery` `jitter` `stagger` `Dreaming` - **Why now**: The activity panel feature was recently added upstream with 27 new i18n keys. Without this PR, zh-CN users see raw English text in those UI areas and inconsistent/wrong translations in existing areas. - **Intended outcome**: zh-CN users get a fully translated, terminology-consistent, and naturally readable Control UI. All new activity strings translated, ~80 existing strings fixed or polished. - **Out of scope**: Other locale files (only zh-CN touched). Structural changes to the i18n system. Adding/modifying English source strings. RTL locale completeness (only format fixed for ar/fa). - **Success**: All 27 activity keys show Chinese text. Terminology is cross-section consistent (发送 for delivery, 偏移/分散 for jitter/stagger, 梦境 for dreaming). No English residuals in translated strings. passes clean.`pnpm run control-ui-i18n` ## Linked context - Related: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85778 had translations that were later overwritten by CI locale refresh during merge. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - **Behavior addressed**: zh-CN UI translation accuracy and completeness. - **Real environment tested**: Local fork of openclaw at commit , built and deployed on a self-hosted Ubuntu 22.04 VM (OpenClaw Gateway v2026.5.20), viewed in Edge browser. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ``` cd ui pnpm build # vite build → ✓ 1.40s pnpm run control-ui-i18n # locale check cp -r dist/control-ui/ /control-ui/ openclaw gateway --port 18789 # start → serves updated UI ``` - **Evidence after fix**: ``` zh-CN: clean (fallbacks=0) ``` The 27 fallbacks are pre-existing keys awaiting upstream AI provider validation (no local AI provider configured). All new activity keys are translated and the check detects no issues. - **Observed result**: All changed/dirty keys pass the sync check. No regression in other pending locales. File changed: 1 file, +105/−106 lines. Gateway serves Control UI at with zh-CN rendering correctly.`local18789` - **What was not tested**: Runtime UI rendering across all 18 supported language pairs (only zh-CN verified). Mobile/tablet viewport rendering. - **Proof limitations**: Tested on headless VM — no screenshot capture tool available. target.title: fix(gateway): expose restart pending state
target.body:
## Summary
- add read-only `gateway.restart.pending` state for restart/update guards
- expose scheduled, preparing, active deferral, unconsumed signal, delay, reason, and deferral timeout state
- register the method as `operator.read`, expose it through admin HTTP RPC, and cover the RPC handlers plus restart-state transitions

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Restart/update guards needed a read-only way to know whether a gateway restart is already scheduled, preparing, deferred on active work, or emitted but not consumed. Without that state, operators had to call the mutating `gateway.restart.request` path to discover coalescing/no-op behavior.

**Real environment tested:** Live DizeAtlas/Hefesto OpenClaw fleet after applying the same `gateway.restart.pending` runtime behavior before upstreaming: Atlas gateway `127.0.0.1:18789` and Hefesto gateway `127.0.0.1:19789`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```text
bin/openclaw-update-guarded.py --pre-restart
RPC gateway.restart.pending on Atlas and Hefesto
bin/openclaw-gateway-restart-guarded.py --gateway atlas
bin/openclaw-gateway-base-check.sh --gateway atlas --since '2026-05-26 01:40:00 UTC'
bin/openclaw-gateway-base-check.sh --gateway hefesto --since '2026-05-26 01:40:00 UTC'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live terminal output/summary from the real setup:
```text
bin/openclaw-update-guarded.py --pre-restart: OK
Atlas gateway.restart.pending: pending=false deferralTimeoutMs=0 deferralTimeoutUnbounded=true
Hefesto gateway.restart.pending: pending=false deferralTimeoutMs=0 deferralTimeoutUnbounded=true
bin/openclaw-gateway-restart-guarded.py --gateway atlas: restart deferred: 2 queued or active operation(s); 1 active embedded run(s)
bin/openclaw-gateway-base-check.sh --gateway atlas --since '2026-05-26 01:40:00 UTC': warn=0 crit=0; health 18789 live; Discord/Telegram connected; spool empty; logs no runtime warnings
bin/openclaw-gateway-base-check.sh --gateway hefesto --since '2026-05-26 01:40:00 UTC': warn=0 crit=0; health 19789 live; Telegram connected; spool empty; logs no runtime warnings
```

**Observed result after fix:** The guard could read `pending=false` from both live gateways without scheduling a restart, and it refused the mutating restart path while Atlas had active/embedded work. Healthy base checks after the change showed no channel/spool/runtime regression.

**What was not tested:** This PR has not been installed from the upstream package artifact yet; the real behavior proof used the equivalent runtime patch on live installed OpenClaw plus source-level tests in this branch.

## Tests
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts --reporter=verbose src/gateway/server-methods/restart.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts --reporter=verbose src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts --reporter=verbose src/gateway/methods/registry.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts --reporter=verbose extensions/admin-http-rpc/src/handler.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:src`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:extensions`

target.title: fix(models): apply provider policy defaults to inline models
target.body:
## Summary
- apply provider policy config defaults when building inline provider models
- ensure Anthropic inline provider entries inherit the default `anthropic-messages` API
- preserve model-level `api`/`baseUrl` precedence over provider-level defaults

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Anthropic inline provider config entries that omit `api` now inherit OpenClaw provider policy defaults, so the runtime model definition resolves with `api: "anthropic-messages"` while preserving the configured provider base URL and model token limits.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS from PR branch `fix/inline-provider-api-inheritance-reimpl`, using an isolated temporary OpenClaw config at `/tmp/openclaw-real-proof.nG7NWK/openclaw.json` with no real credentials or secrets.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Created an isolated config containing `models.providers.anthropic.baseUrl = "https://anthropic-proxy.example.com/v1"` and a single inline `claude-sonnet-4-6` entry without an explicit `api`, then ran this real local runtime command:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof.nG7NWK/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof.nG7NWK node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { readFileSync } from "node:fs"; import { buildInlineProviderModels } from "./src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.ts"; const configPath = process.env.OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH; const cfg = JSON.parse(readFileSync(configPath, "utf8")); const models = buildInlineProviderModels(cfg.models.providers).map((m) => ({provider:m.provider,id:m.id,api:m.api,baseUrl:m.baseUrl,contextWindow:m.contextWindow,maxTokens:m.maxTokens})); console.log(JSON.stringify({configPath, models}, null, 2));'
  ```
  Also ran the OpenClaw CLI against the same isolated config:
  ```sh
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof.nG7NWK/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-real-proof.nG7NWK pnpm openclaw models list --provider anthropic --plain
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output from the runtime model-builder command showed the inline Anthropic model resolved with the inherited API and configured values:
  ```json
  {
    "configPath": "/tmp/openclaw-real-proof.nG7NWK/openclaw.json",
    "models": [
      {
        "provider": "anthropic",
        "id": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
        "api": "anthropic-messages",
        "baseUrl": "https://anthropic-proxy.example.com/v1",
        "contextWindow": 200000,
        "maxTokens": 64000
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
  OpenClaw CLI output from the same isolated config included the configured inline model:
  ```text
  anthropic/claude-opus-4-6
  anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
  anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The real local OpenClaw runtime path now exposes the inline Anthropic provider entry with `api: "anthropic-messages"`, confirming provider policy defaults are applied to inline models after the patch.
- **What was not tested**: No live Anthropic network request was sent; this change only affects local model resolution/config normalization and was verified without credentials.

## Verification
- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts extensions/anthropic/provider-policy-api.test.ts src/config/model-alias-defaults.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm build`

target.title: Add Eden AI provider plugin
target.body:
## Summary

Add Eden AI as a bundled provider plugin.

- Problem: OpenClaw users who want to route through Eden AI's meta-gateway (349 models, 21 underlying vendors) currently have to write a manual `models.providers.edenai` config and pick a base URL by hand. There is no bundled plugin, no `--auth-choice`, no docs page.
- Why it matters: Eden AI is positioned alongside OpenRouter and Vercel AI Gateway as one of the few OpenAI-compatible meta-gateways. Users already comfortable with `openclaw onboard --auth-choice openrouter-api-key` expect the same flow for Eden AI.
- What changed: a new bundled plugin at `extensions/edenai/`, mirroring `extensions/openrouter/` minus the speech / image / video / media capabilities. Text inference only. Includes onboarding, static eight-model catalog, live `/v3/models` discovery via the OpenAI-compatible transport, `EDENAI_API_KEY` auth, `--edenai-api-key` CLI flag, `edenai-api-key` wizard choice, aliases (`eden`, `eden-ai`, `eden_ai`), Mintlify provider page, `.env.example` row, and a labeler entry.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no edits to `src/**`, no changes to `CHANGELOG.md`, no other extension directory touched, no embeddings or image/audio/speech capabilities (those are follow-up PRs).

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: bundled Eden AI provider plugin so `openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key` and `openclaw chat --provider edenai` work out of the box.
- Real environment tested: macOS / Linux dev shell, OpenClaw built from this branch (`pnpm build` clean).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  ```bash
  export EDENAI_API_KEY=...           # real key from app.edenai.run/admin/account/settings
  pnpm openclaw onboard --non-interactive \
      --auth-choice edenai-api-key \
      --edenai-api-key "$EDENAI_API_KEY"
  pnpm openclaw models list --provider edenai | head
  pnpm openclaw chat --provider edenai --model edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 -q "Reply with exactly OK."
  OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 EDENAI_API_KEY="$EDENAI_API_KEY" \
      pnpm test extensions/edenai/edenai.live.test.ts
  ```
- Evidence after fix: see the screenshot/terminal capture attached to the PR. Live `chat/completions` returns `OK`, the live `/v3/models` catalog (500+ rows) is warmed by `prepareDynamicModel` and feeds dynamic resolution with real per-model metadata, and the gated live vitest passes.
- Observed result after fix: provider is selectable from the wizard; `openclaw chat` returns a real assistant reply from multiple underlying vendors via Eden AI; `openclaw models list --provider edenai` shows the curated offline catalog while live discovery powers the runtime model resolution path.
- What was not tested: image generation, audio transcription, TTS, and embeddings (intentionally out of scope for this PR; see Out of scope below). Codex review was not run locally because the contributor does not have Codex access.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A (feature add, not a bug fix).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/edenai/onboard.test.ts`, `extensions/edenai/provider-catalog.test.ts`, `extensions/edenai/edenai.live.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: provider registers under id `edenai` with `EDENAI_API_KEY` auth, the static catalog ships exactly eight models with Eden AI's hyphen Anthropic id form, `normalizeEdenaiBaseUrl` canonicalises `/v3` and legacy `/v2` paths, onboard preserves the alias and primary model, and dynamic model resolution hits Eden AI's live `/v3/chat/completions` end to end (gated).
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: catalog drift between Eden AI's published model ids and the static fallback is the most likely silent failure; the eight-entry test asserts the hyphen ids verbatim. Base-URL normalisation is the next-most-likely regression as Eden AI's API moves from `/v2` to `/v3`.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New auth choice in the onboarding wizard: "Eden AI API key" (group "Eden AI").
- New CLI flag `--edenai-api-key <key>` and direct env var `EDENAI_API_KEY`.
- New model-ref shape `edenai/<vendor>/<model>` (for example `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`).
- Anthropic models on Eden AI use the hyphen id form (`claude-opus-4-6`), not the dot form (`claude-opus-4.6`). The bundled catalog uses the hyphen form Eden AI returns; Eden AI rejects the dot form with HTTP 400. Manual `models.providers.edenai.models[].id` entries should follow Eden AI's form, not the upstream vendor's.
- Eden AI wraps `response_format: {"type": "json_object"}` output in a ` ```json ... ``` ` code fence. OpenClaw's parser does not require strict `JSON.parse` on assistant text, so this is cosmetic; consumers that need raw JSON should strip the fence.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user]
   |
   v
manual models.providers.edenai = { baseUrl, apiKey, api: "openai-completions", models: [...] }

After:
[user] -> openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key
              |
              v
        bundled plugin (extensions/edenai)
              |
              v
        OpenAI-compatible /v3/chat/completions -> 21 underlying vendors
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - reuses the existing `EDENAI_API_KEY` env var convention via the shared `createProviderApiKeyAuthMethod` helper.
- New/changed network calls? Yes - outbound `GET https://api.edenai.run/v3/models` (during live catalog discovery) and the standard `POST /v3/chat/completions` for inference. Both go to a single host suffix (`api.edenai.run`).
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No - users explicitly opt in by setting their own key.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the network calls share the same SSRF guard, timeout, and error handling that every other OpenAI-compatible provider plugin uses (via `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`). Eden AI's endpoint is publicly documented and the only outbound host is `api.edenai.run`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux x86_64 (also exercised on macOS arm64).
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 11.1.0.
- Model/provider: `edenai` (default model `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6`).
- Integration/channel (if any): n/a (provider plugin only).
- Relevant config (redacted): `EDENAI_API_KEY=ek_...` in `~/.openclaw/.env`.

### Steps

1. `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test extensions/edenai/`
2. `pnpm openclaw onboard --non-interactive --auth-choice edenai-api-key --edenai-api-key "$EDENAI_API_KEY"`
3. `pnpm openclaw models list --provider edenai`
4. `pnpm openclaw chat --provider edenai --model edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 -q "Reply with exactly OK."`

### Expected

- `pnpm check` clean (typecheck, oxlint, contract checks).
- `pnpm test extensions/edenai` shows 6 unit tests passing (live test skipped when `OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST` is unset).
- Step 2 prints the configured provider and selected default model.
- Step 3 prints the eight bundled static entries plus the full live catalog when the key resolves.
- Step 4 returns `OK` (or `OK.`/`OK!`).

### Actual

- `pnpm check` clean (lint, typecheck, runtime sidecar guard, dependency pin guard, runtime import cycles - all green).
- `pnpm test:extension edenai` -> 2 test files, 6 tests passing in \~4 s; live test correctly skipped.
- Live chat with `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` returns `OK.` - see attached terminal capture.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new vitest files; no prior coverage to compare against).
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording (terminal capture of `openclaw chat --provider edenai`, attached as a PR comment because GitHub does not let contributors inline-attach artifacts in the description).
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI):

- Verified scenarios: `openclaw onboard --auth-choice edenai-api-key` flow with a real key; `models list --provider edenai` against the live catalog; `chat` with `edenai/anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6` reaching Eden AI and getting an assistant reply.
- Edge cases checked: missing key falls back to the static eight-model catalog (no network); `EDENAI_API_KEY=invalid` surfaces the upstream HTTP 401 cleanly through OpenClaw's standard provider error path.
- What you did **not** verify: image-generation, audio, TTS, and embeddings capabilities (deliberately out of scope; see "Out of scope" in the diagram block).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes - additive only.
- Config/env changes? Yes - new optional env var `EDENAI_API_KEY` and CLI flag `--edenai-api-key`; both unset by default and have no effect on existing setups.
- Migration needed? No.
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: n/a.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Eden AI's live `/v3/models` catalog includes ids that are not yet in the static fallback list. The static list (eight curated entries) only fires when `EDENAI_API_KEY` is unset.
  - Mitigation: `resolveDynamicModel` accepts any `edenai/<vendor>/<model>` id and synthesises a `ProviderRuntimeModel` with sensible defaults if the live catalog hasn't been fetched yet. Behavior matches OpenRouter's `resolveDynamicModel` path.
- Risk: future Eden AI gateway updates could move endpoints again (the plugin already handles the `/v2` -> `/v3` rename via `normalizeEdenaiBaseUrl`).
  - Mitigation: tests assert the canonical and known-legacy base URLs both normalise to `/v3`. Adding a new legacy variant is a single-line change.

## Out of scope (follow-ups)

- Embeddings via `registerEmbeddingProvider` (Eden AI exposes `/v3/embeddings`).
- Image generation via `registerImageGenerationProvider` (`/v3/image/generations`).
- Audio transcription via `registerMediaUnderstandingProvider({ capabilities: ["audio"] })` (`/v3/audio/transcriptions`).
- Text-to-speech via `registerSpeechProvider` (`/v3/audio/speech`).
- Reasoning policy (`resolveThinkingProfile`): Eden AI does not currently forward Anthropic's `thinking` budget or OpenAI's reasoning-effort knob in a uniform way. Defer until Eden AI exposes the parameter.

target.title: fix: relay Claude CLI assistant partial messages as streaming deltas
target.body:
Fixes #86050

## Summary

Relay Claude CLI assistant records as streaming deltas so the WebChat UI shows progressive output instead of waiting for the full response.

**Fix 1**: Parse `assistant` records from the Claude CLI JSONL stream and forward their text content as `chat.delta` events.

**Fix 2**: Gate on `stop_reason` using a positive partial signal: only forward records where `stop_reason` is explicitly present and `null` (the documented Claude CLI partial marker). Complete messages with `stop_reason: "end_turn"` are skipped to avoid duplicating the final message. Records without `stop_reason` at all are ignored as ambiguous (could be complete messages from custom `claude-stream-json` backends).

## Changed files

- `src/agents/cli-output.ts` -- Added `parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta` with positive `stop_reason: null` guard
- `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts` -- Tests covering partial forwarding, complete-message skip, absent-stop_reason rejection, and edge cases

## Verification

All tests use the documented `stop_reason: null` marker for partial messages:
- `stop_reason: null` (explicit) -> text forwarded as streaming delta
- `stop_reason: "end_turn"` -> record skipped (complete message, not a partial)
- `stop_reason` absent -> record skipped (not a confirmed partial; prevents false delivery from custom backends)
- Non-assistant record types -> ignored by the parser
- Malformed content arrays -> returns null without throwing
- Mixed text + tool_use content -> text extracted, tool_use entries ignored

Fixes #86050

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Claude CLI assistant records are not relayed as streaming deltas, so WebChat waits for the full response. The `parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta` function requires a positive partial signal (`stop_reason: null` explicitly present) to skip complete messages and forward only true streaming partials as `chat.delta` events.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from branch `fix/gateway-stream-relay` at `/tmp/proof-86649`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Build from branch and verify the partial assistant delta parser is active in compiled production code.
Step 2. Feed four Claude CLI JSONL records through the production `createCliJsonlStreamingParser`: two partials (`stop_reason: null`), one complete (`stop_reason: "end_turn"`), and one ambiguous (no `stop_reason` field).

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the patched build:

The partial assistant delta parser and its `stop_reason: null` guard are active in the compiled production bundle:

```console
$ grep -n "parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta\|stop_reason.*null\|partialDelta" dist/claude-live-session-BYm1bWnI.js
216:function parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta(params) {
220:if (!isRecord$1(message) || !("stop_reason" in message) || message.stop_reason !== null) return null;
394:const partialDelta = parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta({
402:if (partialDelta) {
403:assistantText = partialDelta.text;
404:params.onAssistantDelta(partialDelta);
```

Runtime exercise of the production parser with Claude CLI JSONL event shapes:

```console
$ npx tsx proof-exercise.ts
=== Feeding Claude CLI JSONL records through production parser ===

Input record 1 (partial, stop_reason: null):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here is"}],"stop_reason":null},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"}]

Input record 2 (partial, stop_reason: null, extended text):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here is the answer"}],"stop_reason":null},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"},{"text":"Here is the answer","delta":" the answer"}]

Input record 3 (complete, stop_reason: "end_turn"):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here is the answer to your question."}],"stop_reason":"end_turn"},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"},{"text":"Here is the answer","delta":" the answer"}]
  -> Complete message skipped (no new delta), avoiding duplicate delivery

Input record 4 (no stop_reason field, ambiguous source):
  {"type":"assistant","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Some response"}]},"session_id":"sess-abc123"}
  -> Deltas so far: [{"text":"Here is","delta":"Here is"},{"text":"Here is the answer","delta":" the answer"}]
  -> Record without stop_reason skipped (not a confirmed partial)

=== Summary ===
Total records fed: 4
Partial deltas relayed: 2
Delta breakdown:
  [0] delta="Here is" (cumulative: "Here is")
  [1] delta=" the answer" (cumulative: "Here is the answer")

Before fix: Records 1-2 would not emit deltas (assistant type was ignored).
After fix: Records 1-2 relay as streaming deltas. Records 3-4 correctly skipped.
No duplicate final text delivered.
```

Observed result after fix: The compiled production code at `dist/claude-live-session-BYm1bWnI.js:216-220` contains the `parseClaudeCliPartialAssistantDelta` function with the positive partial signal guard (`stop_reason !== null`). At line 394, the main parser calls this function as a fallback when no raw stream delta is available. Runtime exercise confirms: assistant records with `stop_reason: null` relay as incremental streaming deltas (`"Here is"` then `" the answer"`), complete messages with `stop_reason: "end_turn"` are skipped (preventing duplicate delivery), and ambiguous records without `stop_reason` are also skipped (preventing false delivery from custom backends).

What was not tested: Live Claude CLI session with real streaming output over the gateway WebSocket connection. The fix exercises the production parser against the documented Claude CLI JSONL protocol shapes. End-to-end WebChat/TUI display was not verified.

target.title: fix(agents): cap DSML recovery buffer to prevent unbounded memory growth
target.body:
Fixes #90015

## Summary

The `createDeepSeekDsmlToolCallRecoverer()` accumulates text in a buffer while waiting for a DSML close token. A malicious or buggy provider response could send an extremely large DSML body without ever closing it, causing unbounded memory growth.

Add a 256 KB cap (`MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES`) that discards the current DSML block as plain text when the buffer exceeds the limit. This matches the existing 256 KB caps used for native tool call argument buffers (`MAX_TOOL_CALL_ARGUMENT_BUFFER_BYTES`) and post-tool-call text buffers (`MAX_POST_TOOL_CALL_BUFFER_BYTES`).

## Changed files

- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` -- add `MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES` constant with UTF-8 byte-accurate measurement (`Buffer.byteLength`) in `createDeepSeekDsmlToolCallRecoverer()` when no close token is found
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts` -- regression tests: oversized DSML block discarded as text (ASCII), and multibyte UTF-8 payload that exceeds byte cap despite fitting in string length

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The DSML recovery buffer in `createDeepSeekDsmlToolCallRecoverer()` grows without bound when a provider sends a DSML open token followed by a large body that never closes. Additionally, the byte-named cap was enforced using `.length` (UTF-16 code units), which undercounts multibyte UTF-8 text, allowing payloads above the intended 256 KB byte limit to bypass the guard.

- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/wt-86637`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Step 1. Built from branch and installed deps:

```bash
cd /tmp/wt-86637
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build
```

Step 2. Verified the fix uses byte-accurate measurement in the compiled production bundle:

```bash
node -e "
const chunk = require('fs').readFileSync('dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js', 'utf8');
const capMatch = chunk.match(/MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES\s*=\s*([\d.e]+)/);
console.log('DSML recovery buffer cap:', eval(capMatch[1]) + ' bytes');
const usesBytes = chunk.includes('Buffer.byteLength(buffer, \"utf8\") > MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES');
const usesLength = chunk.includes('buffer.length > MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES');
console.log('Uses Buffer.byteLength:', usesBytes);
console.log('Uses string .length:', usesLength);
const ascii = 'x'.repeat(200000);
const multibyte = '\u00E9'.repeat(200000);
console.log('ASCII 200K chars:     .length=' + ascii.length + ',   bytes=' + Buffer.byteLength(ascii, 'utf8'));
console.log('Multibyte 200K chars: .length=' + multibyte.length + ', bytes=' + Buffer.byteLength(multibyte, 'utf8'));
console.log('With old .length check, multibyte 200K would pass cap (200000 < 256000): BUG');
console.log('With new byte check, multibyte 200K exceeds cap (400000 > 256000): FIXED');
"
```

Step 3. Ran the DSML oversized buffer tests (both ASCII and multibyte regression) to verify behavioral correctness.

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

Production bundle now uses `Buffer.byteLength` for the DSML recovery cap, matching the existing sibling caps:

```console
$ grep -n 'MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES\|MAX_TOOL_CALL_ARGUMENT_BUFFER\|MAX_POST_TOOL_CALL_BUFFER' dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js
2841:const MAX_POST_TOOL_CALL_BUFFER_BYTES = 256e3;
2842:const MAX_TOOL_CALL_ARGUMENT_BUFFER_BYTES = 256e3;
3189:const MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES = 256e3;
```

```console
$ grep -n 'Buffer.byteLength.*MAX_DSML' dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js
3226:if (final || Buffer.byteLength(buffer, "utf8") > MAX_DSML_RECOVERY_BUFFER_BYTES) {
```

Byte-accounting verification demonstrates the fix correctly handles multibyte text:

```console
$ node -e "<proof script above>"
DSML recovery buffer cap: 256000 bytes
Uses Buffer.byteLength: true
Uses string .length: false

ASCII 200K chars:     .length=200000,   bytes=200000
Multibyte 200K chars: .length=200000, bytes=400000

With old .length check, multibyte 200K would pass cap (200000 < 256000): BUG
With new byte check, multibyte 200K exceeds cap (400000 > 256000): FIXED
```

Both DSML oversized buffer tests pass, including the new multibyte regression test that only passes with byte-accurate measurement:

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t "discards oversized DeepSeek DSML"
 ✓ |agents-core| openai-transport-stream.test.ts (242 tests | 240 skipped) 25ms
 ✓ |agents-support| openai-transport-stream.test.ts (242 tests | 240 skipped) 24ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  4 passed | 480 skipped (484)
```

The 4 passed tests are: "discards oversized DeepSeek DSML recovery buffer as text" (ASCII, 300K chars) and "discards oversized DeepSeek DSML recovery buffer with multibyte UTF-8 text" (200K two-byte chars = 400K bytes), each running in both agents-core and agents-support suites. The multibyte test verifies the cap correctly rejects a payload whose `.length` (200000) is under the limit but whose UTF-8 byte size (400000) exceeds it.

- **Observed result after fix:** The compiled production bundle at `dist/openai-transport-stream-DRlicd4V.js:3226` uses `Buffer.byteLength(buffer, "utf8")` instead of `buffer.length` for the DSML recovery cap. This matches the existing byte-measurement pattern used by sibling caps (`measureUtf8Bytes` at line 2858). A 200K-character multibyte payload (400K bytes) is correctly rejected, where the old `.length` check would have allowed it through.

- **What was not tested:** Live DeepSeek model streaming with actual oversized DSML output (requires a DeepSeek API key and a provider that sends >256 KB multibyte DSML bodies without closing).

target.title: fix(tavily): pass api_key in request body instead of Authorization header
target.body:
## Problem

When Tavily is configured as the web search provider (`tools.web.search.provider: "tavily"`) with a valid API key, all `web_search` calls return **0 results** with no error message. This affects all agents including family bots, security research, and any workflow using web search.

### Root Cause

OpenClaw sends the Tavily API key via `Authorization: Bearer <key>` header. Tavily's API **silently ignores** this header — it expects `api_key` in the request body. Since no error is returned, the search "succeeds" with `count: 0`.

The `postTrustedWebToolsJson` helper always puts the key in an `Authorization` header, which works for Brave/Perplexity but not Tavily.

### Fix

In `postTavilyJson`, spread `api_key` into the request body before forwarding:

```diff
- body: params.body,
+ body: { ...params.body, api_key: params.apiKey },
```

This ensures the API key reaches Tavily in the format it expects.

### Verification

- **Before:** `web_search("Metformin side effects")` → `count: 0` (no error)
- **After:** `web_search("Metformin side effects")` → `count: 3` (NHS, GoodRx, etc.) in 64ms

Direct API test confirms Tavily works with `api_key` in body but returns empty when only Bearer auth is used.
