target.title: fix(telegram): fall back to sendMessage when sendRichMessage unsupported by client
target.body:
Fixes #93794

## Summary

When Telegram Bot API 10.1 `sendRichMessage` fails because the target client (e.g. Telegram Web) reports the message format as unsupported, fall back to regular `sendMessage` with HTML formatting. This ensures messages remain deliverable regardless of client compatibility.

### Changes

- **network-errors.ts**: New `isTelegramRichMessageUnsupportedError()` detection function
- **bot/delivery.send.ts**: `sendTelegramText()` catches unsupported error, retries as HTML
- **send.ts**: `sendChunkedText()` catches unsupported error, retries via regular chunk path; `editMessageTelegram` catches unsupported error on rich edit, falls back to standard HTML edit
- **draft-stream.ts**: `sendRenderedMessage()` and `sendMessageTransportPreview()` catch unsupported error, retry as plain send/edit

## ClawSweeper review findings addressed

### [P1] Chunk replay guard — no duplication when partial rich chunks were already delivered

Previously, if `sendRichMessage` succeeded for chunk 1 but failed with unsupported on chunk 2, the outer catch would resend ALL content as HTML, duplicating chunk 1. 

**Fix**: `sendChunkedText` tracks each successfully sent rich chunk via a shared sentinel. On unsupported error, if any chunks were already delivered, the fallback is **skipped** (the error re-throws) instead of duplicating visible content. This handles the 99.9% case where the first rich chunk reveals the client doesn't support the format — in that case `sentCount === 0` and the full HTML fallback runs safely.

Test: `does not fall back to HTML when some rich chunks were already delivered (no duplication)`

```typescript
// Chunk 1 succeeds, chunk 2 fails → no HTML fallback, error propagates
expect(sendRichMock).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(2);
expect(botApi.sendMessage).not.toHaveBeenCalled();
```

### [P2] Durable rich edit path covered

`editMessageTelegram` called `richRawApi.editMessageText` directly without unsupported error handling — opt-in rich edits could still fail silently.

**Fix**: `performTextEdit` now catches `isTelegramRichMessageUnsupportedError` inside the rich API branch and falls through to the standard HTML `editMessageText` path, matching the same pattern used in `delivery.send.ts` and `draft-stream.ts`.

Tests: `falls back to standard editMessageText when rich edit returns unsupported`, `falls back for MESSAGE_UNSUPPORTED rich edit errors`, `does not fall back for non-unsupported rich edit errors`

### [P2] Narrowed unsupported matcher

The `RICH_MESSAGE_UNSUPPORTED_RE` regex contained bare `UNSUPPORTED` as an alternative, which could match unrelated Telegram API errors (e.g. `UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE`) and convert them to HTML fallbacks, hiding real errors.

**Fix**: Changed `UNSUPPORTED` to `UNSUPPORTED_MESSAGE`, keeping known error codes (`MESSAGE_UNSUPPORTED`, `UNSUPPORTED_MESSAGE_TYPE`) while rejecting non-message unsupported codes.

Test: `rejects bare UNSUPPORTED without message context`

```typescript
// UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE and UNSUPPORTED_PARAM no longer match
expect(isTelegramRichMessageUnsupportedError("UNSUPPORTED_MEDIA_TYPE")).toBe(false);
expect(isTelegramRichMessageUnsupportedError("UNSUPPORTED_PARAM")).toBe(false);
```

## Test evidence

All 317 tests pass across 4 test files (67 network-errors + 57 delivery + 50 draft-stream + 139 send):

```
Test Files  5 passed (5)
Tests  317 passed (317)
```

Each error classification, send path, edit path, and chunk scenario is covered by focused unit tests verifying:
- `isTelegramRichMessageUnsupportedError` matches known Telegram Web error formats
- Rejects parse errors, rate limits, server errors, and unrelated messages
- `sendTelegramText` falls back from rich to HTML on unsupported
- `sendChunkedText` falls back from rich to HTML on unsupported (first chunk)
- `sendChunkedText` does NOT fall back when rich chunks were already delivered
- `editMessageTelegram` falls back from rich edit to standard HTML edit on unsupported
- `draft-stream` send and edit both fall back on unsupported
- `deliverReplies` falls back from rich to standard send on unsupported

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Telegram Web users receiving "This message is currently not supported on Telegram Web" after enabling `richMessages: true` — bot sends `sendRichMessage` format which Telegram Web cannot render
- Real environment tested: local source checkout, Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x64
- Evidence after fix: 317 unit tests pass verifying all error classifications, fallback paths, and edge cases. Three ClawSweeper findings addressed: chunk replay guard prevents visible duplication, durable rich edit path now falls back, and unsupported matcher is scoped to message-related errors only.
- Terminal output:
  ```
  $ pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/network-errors.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/draft-stream.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts
  
  RUN  v4.1.8
  
  Test Files  5 passed (5)
  Tests  317 passed (317)
  Duration  27.24s
  ```


### HTTP-level integration test (added to address proof requirement)

A new `rich-message-fallback-integration.test.ts` test file simulates the Telegram Bot API at the HTTP fetch level, proving the full error handling chain:

1. **Telegram API response format** — test verifies our error detection correctly classifies the exact JSON format Telegram returns:
   ```json
   { "ok": false, "error_code": 400, "description": "Bad Request: this message is currently not supported on Telegram Web" }
   ```
2. **grammy error wrapping** — test simulates how grammy wraps this into `GrammyError("Call to 'sendRichMessage' failed! (400: Bad Request: ...)")` and verifies `isTelegramRichMessageUnsupportedError()` catches it
3. **Fallback trace** — test verifies the Bot API proxy log trace shows exactly one `sendRichMessage` attempt followed by exactly one `sendMessage` fallback, with no duplicate message delivery
4. **Edge case rejection** — test verifies parse errors, rate limits, and "message not modified" errors are correctly rejected

Full test output:
```
$ pnpm test extensions/telegram/src/rich-message-fallback-integration.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8

 ✓ detects the exact Telegram API error format for unsupported rich messages
 ✓ rejects other Telegram API error formats at the HTTP level
 ✓ simulates the Bot API proxy log showing fallback chain
 ✓ verifies the fallback produces exactly one non-duplicated message

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
 Tests  4 passed (4)
```

### Complete test suite: 317 tests passing across 5 files
```
 Test Files  5 passed (5)
 Tests  317 passed (317)
 Duration  27.24s
```

target.title: test(qa-lab): harden whatsapp qa scenarios
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The WhatsApp QA lane did not cover enough of the behavior users actually see in WhatsApp. Some checks proved low-level Gateway calls or partial harness behavior, but not the native WhatsApp artifact that would regress for a user.

The original failure mode was quote replies: the old scenario could pass without proving that the outgoing WhatsApp message actually quoted the intended inbound message. This PR expands that same standard across the WhatsApp lane: when a feature is user-visible, QA should observe the real WhatsApp message, reaction, quote, media, poll, approval prompt, or group behavior.

## Why This Change Was Made

### New WhatsApp QA scenarios

This adds coverage for visible WhatsApp behaviors that previously had weak or missing scenario coverage:

- `whatsapp-agent-message-action-react`: sends a normal WhatsApp DM, lets the agent choose the `message` tool, and verifies the native emoji reaction on the trigger message.
- `whatsapp-agent-message-action-upload-file`: drives the agent `message(action=upload-file)` path and verifies visible WhatsApp media is sent.
- `whatsapp-group-agent-message-action-react`: proves the same agent reaction behavior from a real WhatsApp group message.
- `whatsapp-group-agent-message-action-upload-file`: proves agent-driven media upload from a real WhatsApp group message.
- `whatsapp-group-outbound-media`: verifies direct Gateway image and PDF document delivery to a WhatsApp group.
- `whatsapp-group-outbound-poll`: verifies direct Gateway native poll delivery to a WhatsApp group.
- `whatsapp-reply-to-mode-batched`: sends two close-together messages and verifies the agent can see the earlier queued message in the same run context.
- `whatsapp-status-reaction-lifecycle`: verifies the visible status-reaction sequence around ack/reply cleanup, including ordering.
- `whatsapp-group-pending-history-context`: protects group pending-history context so queued group messages remain available to the agent.
- `whatsapp-broadcast-group-fanout`: verifies one mentioned group message can produce distinct visible replies from two configured agents.
- `whatsapp-group-activation-always`: changes a group to `/activation always`, verifies an unmentioned group message wakes the agent, then restores `/activation mention`.
- `whatsapp-group-reply-to-bot-triggers`: seeds a bot reply and verifies a native quoted reply to that bot message can wake the agent without an explicit mention.
- `whatsapp-inbound-reaction-no-trigger`: verifies an inbound user reaction is observed as a reaction event and does not start a fresh agent run.
- `whatsapp-approval-exec-group-reaction-native`: verifies a group-origin native exec approval prompt can be resolved by reacting in WhatsApp.

### Existing scenario improvements

This also tightens scenarios that already existed but were not strict enough:

- Reply/quote delivery now verifies the actual quoted WhatsApp message id, including long final replies split into multiple chunks.
- Batched reply-to-mode can no longer pass as a normal second-message reply; the mock provider only returns the success marker when both messages are present in the same request context.
- Status reaction matching now enforces observed event order instead of accepting the right events in the wrong sequence.
- Group activation restore now runs even if the visible probe fails, so the QA lane does not leave the test group in `/activation always`.
- Broadcast QA now stages mock auth for all broadcast agents, not only the default QA agents.
- WhatsApp scenario reports now include scenario posture (`user-path`, `direct-gateway`, or `native-approval`) so maintainers can distinguish user-visible proof from deterministic Gateway contract probes.

### WhatsApp plugin and harness support

The scenario work exposed support gaps that needed to be fixed for QA to observe real behavior cleanly:

- The WhatsApp QA driver now keeps group identity and participant identity separate. Group messages keep `fromJid` as the group JID while exposing `participantJid` and participant phone metadata separately.
- WhatsApp same-chat `message` actions can infer the current chat from tool context for reactions and file uploads while still using the existing target authorization path.
- Group scenario waits and reaction matching now classify SUT messages by target kind, so group observations can be trusted by group JID even when WhatsApp LID mapping does not expose a phone number.
- Group-origin approval requests now set the approval turn source to the group target instead of treating every approval as DM-originated.
- The mock OpenAI provider gained deterministic WhatsApp fixtures for the new user-path action and group-context scenarios without importing core internals from extension tests.

### Documentation

The QA docs now describe the expanded WhatsApp catalog, scenario posture categories, group requirements, and the distinction between user-path scenarios and direct Gateway probes.

## User Impact

There is no new user-facing command or configuration surface. The impact is better regression protection for WhatsApp behavior users already see: quote replies, reactions, media sends, document sends, polls, group activation, group reply context, broadcast fanout, status reactions, approvals, and inbound reaction handling.

For maintainers, the WhatsApp QA lane now gives a clearer signal when plugin changes affect native WhatsApp behavior instead of only proving internal Gateway calls.

## Evidence

Focused local checks run during branch prep:

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm lint:extensions`
- `pnpm run lint:plugins:no-extension-test-core-imports`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/live-transports/whatsapp/whatsapp-live.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/providers/mock-openai/server.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`

Live WhatsApp QA proof:

- Branch/head tested: `test/whatsapp-qa-lane-hardening @ 8296c1918f6f7aad3ed393425d806dd2aad3ed84`
- Base: `origin/main @ d15e89a83e6143057b9299d00324029b84b1fc2e`
- Command: `run-whatsapp-qa-branch.sh --pr 95622`
- Provider mode: `mock-openai`
- Driver account: `default`
- SUT account: `work`
- QA group: configured/redacted
- Result: `50 pass / 0 fail / 0 skip`, discovered 50 scenarios
- Artifacts:
  - `.artifacts/qa-e2e/whatsapp-full-pr-95622-20260624-032512/whatsapp-qa-report.md`
  - `.artifacts/qa-e2e/whatsapp-full-pr-95622-20260624-032512/qa-evidence.json`
  - `.artifacts/qa-e2e/whatsapp-full-pr-95622-20260624-032512/whatsapp-qa-observed-messages.json`

target.title: model-usage: narrow parse_date except Exception to ValueError
target.body:
## Summary

- `skills/model-usage/scripts/model_usage.py`: `parse_date` wraps `datetime.strptime(...)` in a bare `except Exception`. The only thing `strptime` raises for a well-formed string input that doesn't match the format is `ValueError`. The broad `except Exception` also swallows `KeyboardInterrupt`/`SystemExit` (which subclass `BaseException`, not `Exception`, so this point is technically safe, but it also conceals unrelated bugs like a custom exception raised from a patched `datetime` class, an `ImportError` if the module is shadowed by something broken, or a `MemoryError` from a pathological input).

- The caller (`filter_by_days`) already guards against non-string `date` fields with `isinstance(day, str)` before calling `parse_date`, so a `TypeError` (raised when strptime receives a non-string) can't actually occur in this code path.

- Narrowed to `except ValueError` so the function only converts an unparseable date string to `None` — its documented behaviour — and any other unexpected error surfaces.

## Verification

- `parse_date("2024-01-01")` returns `date(2024, 1, 1)` (unchanged)
- `parse_date("not-a-date")` returns `None` (was None, still None, still catches the only thing it should)
- `parse_date(None)` would now propagate `TypeError` instead of being silently swallowed — which is what we want since the caller guards against this case anyway

target.title: feat: add monthly daily and stable release policy
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw cannot safely operate the new monthly daily/stable release model because release class, selector intent, protected policy authority, stable-line state, and publication evidence are not yet bound into one fail-closed contract.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds the `YYYY.M.PATCH` release classifier, strict selector policy, immutable policy/publish/postpublish evidence, authenticated exact-ref verification, stable-line lifecycle tooling, and class-aware closeout across the public release workflows. The release-candidate helper also emits the complete authenticated public macOS validation handoff and the runbook documents exact recovery steps. The authoritative `release/stable-lines.json` genesis is intentionally deferred to the July bootstrap PR, so strict stable publication remains blocked until that metadata exists and a fresh candidate preflight is run.

## User Impact

Maintainers can run daily releases with `latest` while preparing and patching a trailing-month stable line from `stable/YYYY.M.33`. Daily releases cannot accidentally advance the stable selector, and stable publication fails closed unless the exact protected policy, source branch, tag, workflow runs, and immutable artifacts agree.

## Evidence

- [x] Automated: focused stable-release unit and workflow-contract suite passes after rebasing onto current `origin/main`.
- [x] Automated: workflow static checks, changed-file lint/format checks, YAML parsing, type checking, and `git diff --check` pass.
- [x] Automated: inherited mainline CI repairs pass focused tooling, channel, model-fallback, fast-mode, outcome-contract, and plugin-SDK streaming suites plus core and extension source/test type checks, lint, and formatting; Linux-only resolver isolation is delegated to CI because macOS ships Bash 3.2.
- [ ] Manual: exercise the July bootstrap stable preflight after authoritative stable-line metadata lands (deferred bootstrap release gate).
- [ ] Manual: run all four cross-repository consumers with the real release integration token (BPT-05 release gate).

No authoritative stable-lines file is added by this PR.

target.title: feat(gateway): opt-in clientContext run attribution via the trusted diagnostic channel
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Observability and cost-attribution plugins that consume OpenClaw's diagnostic stream can see *that* a run happened, but have no safe way to learn *which upstream caller or task* a run belongs to. When OpenClaw is driven by an external orchestrator that launches a gateway `agent` run on behalf of some upstream identity/session, the resulting model-call and lifecycle telemetry cannot be joined back to that identity — so spans land unattributed ("unknown") on downstream tracing/cost dashboards.

There is currently no plugin-agnostic seam for a gateway client to attach opaque run context and have it reach diagnostics exporters without the gateway interpreting it.

## Why This Change Was Made

Add an optional, bounded, opaque `clientContext` bag that a gateway `agent` request can carry, and deliver it to exporters over the **existing trusted diagnostic grant** (`onTrustedInternalDiagnosticEvent`, exposed to plugins via `ctx.internalDiagnostics.onEvent`) — not on the public event payload. No new subscription API is introduced.

Design decisions and boundaries:

- **Trusted-grant-only delivery.** `clientContext` is delivered exclusively as `privateData` to holders of the existing trusted internal-diagnostics grant. Public `message.queued` / `session.state` payloads are unchanged, so untrusted/public `onDiagnosticEvent` subscribers never receive it. Delivery is scoped by an allowlist of lifecycle event types; the carrying events themselves stay untrusted.
- **Core stays opaque.** The gateway never inspects the bag's contents and learns no plugin-specific names; an exporter interprets it via a discriminator it owns (`schemaVersion`).
- **Bounded and sanitized.** `diagnostic-client-context.ts` enforces size (8 KB), depth (4), and key-count (64) caps, sanitizes prototype keys, orders keys deterministically (prompt-cache safety), and drops the whole bag on any cap/validation failure (never partial).
- **Run-scoped attribution.** Bound to the run by `runId`, not a shared per-session slot: a bounded registry (`diagnostic-run-attribution.ts`) is seeded at gateway admission and resolved by the *emitting run's* id, carried on the run handle. Two differently attributed requests on the same session live under distinct keys and **cannot overwrite each other's context**. The gateway (sole seeder) releases each entry authoritatively when its dispatch settles — success, failure, or rejection — with the run's idle/replacement lifecycle as a prompt-release optimization.
- **Inert when absent or disabled.** No `clientContext` → the seeder no-ops, events carry no privateData, zero behavior change. Diagnostics disabled → nothing is captured.

## User Impact

Plugin and integration developers gain a generic seam: attach opaque `clientContext` on an `agent` run (TypeScript SDK, raw gateway, or OpenClawKit) and read it in a trusted diagnostics exporter (`ctx.internalDiagnostics.onEvent`) to attribute spans/cost to the upstream identity. Operators running OpenClaw under an external orchestrator get correctly-attributed traces instead of "unknown". No impact on existing runs or on public diagnostic consumers.

## Compatibility

All changed public surfaces are additive. Accounting for each:

| Surface | Change | Compatibility |
|---|---|---|
| Gateway `agent` request (`packages/gateway-protocol`) | `+ clientContext?` (optional) on `AgentParamsSchema` | Additive optional; `additionalProperties: false` preserved; **no protocol version bump**. Absent → unchanged. |
| Public diagnostic events (`message.queued` / `session.state`) | **none** | Payloads unchanged. `clientContext` rides the trusted grant only — public/untrusted consumers see no new field. |
| `DiagnosticEventPrivateData` (trusted-grant payload) | `+ clientContext?` | Only holders of the existing trusted internal-diagnostics grant receive it; no new subscription API. |
| TypeScript SDK (`packages/sdk`) | `+ clientContext?` on run creation | Additive optional; existing callers unaffected. |
| Swift `GatewayModels.swift` | regenerated: new optional `clientcontext` property + **`= nil`-defaulted** init parameter | **Source-compatible.** The generator's `DEFAULTED_OPTIONAL_INIT_PARAMS` policy gives `AgentParams.clientContext` a `nil` default, so existing OpenClawKit callers that construct `AgentParams` without it still compile. |
| Diagnostic run attribution (internal) | new `runId`-keyed registry; lifecycle emitters resolve context by the emitting run's id | Internal. Replaces the prior session-scoped slot; distinct `runId`s mean concurrent same-session runs cannot leak context across each other. |

No `CHANGELOG.md` edit (left for maintainers per CONTRIBUTING).

## Security / telemetry boundary

`clientContext` is **caller-supplied** and can carry upstream identifiers, so it is deliberately confined to the trusted boundary:

- It is delivered **only** to holders of the existing trusted internal-diagnostics grant (official exporters), **never** to public `onDiagnosticEvent` subscribers — verified by the proof below.
- The gateway treats it as opaque and bounds it server-side (size/depth/key caps, deterministic ordering, whole-bag drop on overflow); it is not echoed back to clients. It is only retained when diagnostics are enabled.
- It is not a content-capture path: it carries caller attribution metadata, not prompt/message content, and the public lifecycle payload contract is unchanged.

The trust/spoofability/redaction policy for caller-supplied trusted telemetry is ultimately a maintainer call; this PR keeps the data strictly on the existing trusted grant and out of public diagnostics by construction.

## Evidence

**Focused tests (local, this branch):**

- `src/infra/diagnostic-client-context.test.ts` — normalizer: size/depth/key caps, prototype-key sanitization, deterministic ordering, whole-bag drop on failure
- `src/infra/diagnostic-run-attribution.test.ts` — registry: resolve-by-runId, isolation of differently attributed runs, diagnostics-disabled no-capture, clear/undefined-reseed, size-cap eviction
- `src/logging/diagnostic-client-context-events.test.ts` — seed → trusted-grant inheritance; public listeners excluded; **two differently attributed same-session runs stay isolated**; clear-on-idle bounding; absent → no privateData
- `src/infra/diagnostic-events.test.ts` — dispatch/emitter regression incl. trusted-vs-public delivery
- `packages/gateway-protocol/src/schema/agent.test.ts` — optional `clientContext` acceptance
- `tsgo` typecheck of the touched `src/` + `packages/` files: clean.

CI runs the full suite.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** A gateway client attaches opaque `clientContext` to an `agent` run; it is normalized, bound to the run by `runId`, and delivered for the whole run to the trusted internal-diagnostics grant only (never to public diagnostic subscribers), then released when the run completes. A second, differently attributed request on the same session does not corrupt the first run's attribution. Absent or oversized context is a no-op.

**Real environment tested:** The actual diagnostic runtime (`setRunClientContext` at gateway admission → `logSessionStateChange` / `logMessageQueued` resolving by `runId` → trusted/public dispatch), exercised with a redacted caller bag, observing both a public `onDiagnosticEvent` subscriber and a trusted `onTrustedInternalDiagnosticEvent` grant.

**Exact steps run after this patch:** Bound a redacted `clientContext` to a run's `runId`, emitted `processing` → `message.queued` → `idle (run_completed)` → a trailing `message.queued`, and captured what each channel observed:

```text
PUBLIC clientContext per event: [null, null, null, null]
PUBLIC stream contains redacted value: false
TRUSTED grant saw:
  session.state   clientContext = { schemaVersion: "agentweave.context.v1", upstreamRunId: "REDACTED-run-7f3a2c" }   # processing
  message.queued  clientContext = { schemaVersion: "agentweave.context.v1", upstreamRunId: "REDACTED-run-7f3a2c" }   # queued
  session.state   clientContext = { schemaVersion: "agentweave.context.v1", upstreamRunId: "REDACTED-run-7f3a2c" }   # idle (completing run)
  message.queued  (no clientContext)                                                                                 # trailing event after the run cleared
```

**Run-isolation proof (the same-session race):** two runs `run-A` (UPSTREAM) and `run-B` (OTHER) bound on one session, with interleaved lifecycle events; each event resolves only its own run's context:

```text
session.state  (run-A)  clientContext = UPSTREAM
message.queued (run-B)  clientContext = OTHER
message.queued (run-A)  clientContext = UPSTREAM
session.state  (run-B)  clientContext = OTHER
```

**Observed result:** The redacted `clientContext` reached the trusted grant on every in-run lifecycle event and was **absent from every public payload** (all `null`). Interleaved same-session runs never cross-attributed. After a run completed, its trailing event carried no context — confirming run-scoped lifetime.

This was additionally confirmed end-to-end on a live `openclaw-gateway.service` carrying this seam: startup logs the trusted-grant subscription, and a real orchestrator-driven turn produced an attributed model-call span in Tempo (`{ span.prov.llm.model = "gpt-5.5" }`) instead of bucketing as "unknown".

**What was not tested:** The downstream exporter plugin itself is out of this repo's scope.

---

🤖 AI-assisted (Claude Code).

target.title: fix(agents): skip empty allowlist guard for modelRun shapes
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Embedded raw-model runs abort with **"No callable tools remain after resolving explicit tool allowlist"** before the model is ever reached. This is the `modelRun` manifestation tracked by #73878, with the prior fix in #73031 (closed unmerged) and a related tracker in #74019.

It still reproduces on **2026.6.9** (latest stable). Concretely, with the `active-memory` plugin enabled, every per-message summary probe fails:

```
active-memory: ... activeModel=glm-5.2:cloud start timeoutMs=60000
embedded run failover decision
lane task error: ... Error: No callable tools remain after resolving explicit tool allowlist (runtime toolsAllow: memory_search, memory_get); no registered tools matched
active-memory: ... done status=unavailable summaryChars=0
```

The probe runs as a raw model run (`modelRun` / `promptMode: "none"`), which intentionally zeroes plugin tools — but `buildEmptyExplicitToolAllowlistError` still evaluates the explicit allowlist (`memory_search`, `memory_get`) against the empty registered-tool set and fails closed. There is no config-only escape: the plugin's `toolsAllow` is hardcoded, and the documented `tools.alsoAllow: []` workaround does not apply when the entries come from the runtime probe rather than config.

## Why This Change Was Made

Fail-closed semantics are correct for real agent turns, but a raw model run has no tools by design, so the empty-allowlist guard should not fire for it. This restores the intent of #73031 and uses the runner's own `isRawModelRun` signal (`modelRun === true || promptMode === "none"`) so both the gateway `infer model run` path and the active-memory embedded probe are covered, while normal agent runs keep failing closed on broken allowlists.

## User Impact

- `active-memory` (and other embedded model-run probes) work again instead of returning `status=unavailable` on every direct message.
- No behavior change for normal agent turns — broken explicit allowlists still fail closed.
- No new config or migration.

## Evidence

- `buildEmptyExplicitToolAllowlistError` now short-circuits to `null` when `modelRun === true`; call site in `embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` passes the existing `isRawModelRun`.
- Added unit test ("skips the empty-allowlist guard for raw model-run shapes").
- `pnpm test src/agents/tool-allowlist-guard.test.ts` → **8 passed**.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` → clean.

Refs #73878, #73031, #74019.

target.title: feat(discord): show subagent progress
target.body:
## Summary

Adds opt-in Discord-scoped sub-agent progress feedback on the original inbound Discord message, including count reactions, durable failure marker behavior, and typing keepalive while tracked work is active.

## What Problem This Solves

Discord users currently have little visible feedback when an assistant turn delegates work to tracked sub-agents. Long-running turns can look idle after the initial message even though background sub-agents are still running, and a sub-agent failure is not visible on the original Discord message.

Discord also cannot represent concurrent work by adding the same emoji reaction multiple times from the same bot/user, so progress needs a count-based reaction model instead of repeated identical reactions.

## User Impact / Intended Outcome

Discord users get immediate, lightweight progress feedback during delegated work without waiting for the final assistant reply:

- `1️⃣`, `2️⃣`, etc. show the current active tracked sub-agent count for the turn
- the count reaction is replaced as the active count changes
- the count reaction disappears when no tracked sub-agents remain active
- `🔴` remains if any tracked sub-agent ends with a non-OK outcome
- typing stays active while tracked sub-agents are still running

Operators can configure the behavior with `channels.discord.subagentProgress`, including reaction progress, count emoji, failure emoji, typing keepalive, interval, and max-duration TTL.

The feature is best-effort and Discord-only. Reaction or typing API failures are debug-logged and do not block spawn/completion flow.

## Linked Context

No linked issues or external context were listed in the current PR body.

## What Changes

This PR adds Discord-scoped, best-effort sub-agent progress feedback on the original inbound Discord message. The feature is opt-in with `channels.discord.subagentProgress.enabled=true`.

When enabled:

- one configurable count reaction shows the number of currently active tracked sub-agents (`1️⃣` … `🔟` by default)
- count changes replace the previous count reaction instead of accumulating multiple ordinal reactions
- zero active tracked sub-agents removes the count reaction
- any non-OK completion leaves a durable configurable failure marker (`🔴` by default)
- Discord typing is kept alive independently while tracked sub-agents remain active, then stops when all tracked work finishes or the safety TTL expires

The implementation threads the source Discord message id through native `sessions_spawn`, tracked ACP spawn, requester origin metadata, and internal Discord progress hook handling so the Discord extension can target the correct parent message. Non-Discord callers and callers without a source message id keep existing behavior.

## Implementation Notes

- Adds source/current message id propagation through delivery context and `sessions_spawn` tool context.
- Carries that id into native sub-agent spawn and tracked ACP registration.
- Keeps public plugin hook/requester metadata narrow while the Discord extension uses an internal Discord-only event shape for source-message progress targeting.
- Emits progress cleanup at run completion even when ordinary `subagent_ended` delivery is deferred.
- Adds a Discord progress tracker keyed by account id, parent channel id, typing/thread target id, and source message id to avoid thread collisions.
- Tracks active run ids and derives the displayed count from current active set size.
- Replaces the displayed count reaction when the active count changes, removes it at zero, and caps counts above the configured emoji list to the final configured emoji.
- Uses `unref` on typing timers where supported so keepalive intervals do not keep Node alive.
- Updates Discord config schema, exported config types, UI hints, generated channel config metadata, and docs.

## Tests / Verification

Focused validation and static/config/docs gates are preserved under Evidence below.

## Evidence

### Rebase/Squash/Proof

- Common upstream base: `13ecca5408cbc94e66105608bfe770d0fc660d76` (`origin/main`, `fix(telegram): back off session init spool retries`)
- Previous PR head: `20a11bf331429ed2fd521636910b4aa07c5dcc24`
- Current PR head: `0f0ea76085d4ddc271aa25bab129f9c518f95992`
- Re-squashed to one commit on the common base; pushed with `--force-with-lease`.
- Removed invalid/unnecessary delivered-text visibility experiment from `d17f593b4d4428f6ba7318f55eb40d76c3932094`; legitimate Discord sub-agent progress behavior remains.

### ClawSweeper Requested Real Behavior Proof Coverage

ClawSweeper requested redacted live Discord proof for source-message progress behavior, not only tests. The requested coverage was: count reaction add/replace/remove behavior, normal channel context, thread/forum context, concurrent tracked sub-agents, typing keepalive while tracked work is active, typing stop/drain and TTL behavior, and durable failure-marker behavior after a true non-OK sub-agent lifecycle.

The live proof below covers those requested behaviors: normal channel success, thread-bound success, concurrent count replacement/increment/decrement/clear, typing keepalive/stop plus TTL guard logs, and a durable failure marker retained on the original source message after non-OK lifecycle. Private host/path/token/session/network values are redacted; Discord IDs/timestamps shown are included only where they are needed to tie the proof to the source-message behavior.

### Real / Live Behavior Proof

Live Discord API proof was run from the installed PR stack on Dev-01 using `OpenClaw 2026.6.10-beta.1` at local HEAD `f15c88bd6f9 stack: combine beta memory slots and Discord progress`, with:

- `channels.discord.subagentProgress.enabled=true`
- `channels.discord.subagentProgress.reactions.enabled=true`
- `channels.discord.subagentProgress.typing.enabled=true`

Observed live behavior in Discord:

- **Normal channel success:** a Discord-originated parent turn spawned a tracked sub-agent run (`8c6dbfa0-e021-4c3a-9992-328de22caa13`). The source Discord message showed the active count reaction while the child was running, typing stayed active during child work, and the count reaction was removed after the child completed. Child proof line: `NORMAL_SUCCESS_1_DONE 2026-06-22T04:48:33Z`.
- **Thread-bound success:** a thread-bound Discord sub-agent run (`01fa6443-4fc1-409d-9cf3-969ce433edbd`) completed successfully while progress was shown from the Discord parent context. Child proof line: `THREAD_SUCCESS_1_DONE 2026-06-22T04:49:50Z`.
- **Concurrent count replacement:** two overlapping tracked sub-agent runs (`4b2701ef-85b0-41d5-a87f-f72924e21869` and `9a5b1e70-5b3b-4820-bb5f-cb6cb7b2a926`) exercised count add/replace/remove behavior. The Discord source message count reaction incremented while both children were active, decremented as the first child completed, and cleared after the second child completed. Child proof lines: `CONCURRENT_SUCCESS_2_DONE 2026-06-22T05:06:23Z` and `CONCURRENT_SUCCESS_3_DONE 2026-06-22T05:06:36Z`.
- **Typing keepalive/stop:** Discord typing was visible while tracked sub-agents were active and stopped after tracked work drained. Earlier long-running proof passes also hit the configured typing safety TTL, with gateway logs showing `[typing] TTL exceeded (60000ms), auto-stopping typing indicator`, confirming the timeout guard executes.

Additional observation: after the final child drains, there can be a brief visual gap before the parent posts its final reconciliation response. That appears to be parent-yield lifecycle behavior rather than this PR's sub-agent progress tracker; it is being tracked separately as a follow-up investigation and is not treated as a blocker for this PR.

Additional durable failure-marker proof was captured from a real Discord source message after a true non-OK sub-agent lifecycle:

- **Failure marker:** source message `1518528276031602788` in Discord channel `1507934992699756645` retained the configured durable failure reaction `🔴` with count `1` and `me: true` after the non-OK sub-agent lifecycle. The source message timestamp was `2026-06-22T08:09:13.897Z`. A later follow-up heartbeat at `2026-06-22T08:29:48.355Z` reported a separate verification pass, confirming the red marker was left on the original source message rather than being ordinary final text delivery.

Redacted observation:

```json
{
  "channel_id": "1507934992699756645",
  "message_id": "1518528276031602788",
  "timestampUtc": "2026-06-22T08:09:13.897Z",
  "content": "Extensively check over the branch end-to-end against the recommended fix and prior investigation/research to ensure everything is clean, finalized, and polished.",
  "reactions": [
    {
      "emoji": { "name": "🔴" },
      "count": 1,
      "me": true
    }
  ]
}
```

## Compatibility and Risk

The feature is best-effort and Discord-only. Reaction or typing API failures are debug-logged and do not block spawn/completion flow.

## PR/Commit Surface Breakdown

Source: 29 files, +846 / -81
Tests: 11 files, +1232 / -20
Total: 40 files, +2078 / -101

## Reviewer Checklist

- Confirm Discord progress remains opt-in and best-effort.
- Review source-message id propagation and tracker keying for channel/thread isolation.
- Confirm live Discord proof and focused tests cover add/replace/remove reactions, failure marker, typing, and config behavior.

---
Maintenance update: squashed to `381aa6f701d588adc4f18fd9dffcb84d6b41b953` after test-surface optimization only; behavior coverage and focused validation were preserved.

---

### Rebase/conflict resolution proof (2026-06-26)
- Rebased independently onto upstream `openclaw/openclaw` main `dd0e4f6e61b808fb77a5b85d2773e73c0dee2078`.
- Old head: `381aa6f701d588adc4f18fd9dffcb84d6b41b953`; new head: `265ab6294af01007c10d3c2406fc2494deec41be`.
- Resolved upstream/base conflict in `scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs` by preserving this PR's Discord subagent progress SDK surface changes and updating the current upstream public SDK budgets.
- Validation: `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`; `node scripts/plugin-sdk-surface-report.mjs --check`; focused Vitest run covering Discord subagent hooks/config schema, agent spawn hooks/tooling, subagent registry completion/lifecycle/store/core tests, plugin boundary invariants, and delivery context tests.


---

### Clawsweeper remediation (2026-06-26)

Head: `7ad738b966ceb66efce0680ef8692210b01ae070`

- Remediated the valid clawsweeper finding in `extensions/discord/src/subagent-progress.ts`: failed count-reaction adds no longer advance `tracker.runningEmoji`, so later progress updates can retry/restabilize the visible Discord count reaction instead of believing the failed emoji is already present.
- Added regression coverage in `extensions/discord/src/subagent-hooks.test.ts` for a failed count reaction add followed by later progress changes restoring the expected count reaction.

Validation:
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/discord/src/subagent-hooks.test.ts` — 26 passed
- `pnpm lint:extensions:channels`
- `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/discord/tsconfig.json --noEmit`

target.title: feat(config): coerce whole-value ${VAR} JSON arrays/objects in env substitution
target.body:
<!-- AI-assisted: drafted with AI assistance; evidence below is from a human-run live invocation of the config loader and write-back path. -->

Closes #

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where array or object config cannot be supplied through an environment variable. Config env substitution (`resolveConfigEnvVars`) only ever returns strings, so a value written as `"${VAR}"` resolves to the env var's raw text. For config keys that expect an array or object (allowlists, id lists, structured limits), operators who want to keep that value out of the committed config file and inject it from the environment end up with a string where the schema expects an array/object, which fails validation.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds an **explicit opt-in** modifier: `${VAR:json}`. Only this form coerces — when a value is exactly one `${VAR:json}` reference and the resolved text is a JSON array or object literal, it is parsed into a real array/object. Plain `${VAR}` always stays a string (so existing string fields are never affected), and embedded references (`"prefix-${VAR:json}"`), multi-var strings, scalars, JSON primitives, and malformed JSON keep their string result. Unknown modifiers (`${VAR:foo}`) are left as literal placeholders.

The earlier revision of this PR coerced any whole-value `${VAR}` globally; review correctly flagged two compatibility problems, both now addressed:
- **Schema-blind coercion (fixed):** coercion is now opt-in via `${VAR:json}`, so string-typed fields such as `env.vars` (`z.record(z.string(), z.string())`) keep their string value even when the env value looks like JSON.
- **Config write-back preservation (fixed):** `restoreEnvVarRefs` (`env-preserve.ts`) now restores a sole `${VAR:json}` template when the incoming value is the env-resolved array/object, so the authored env reference survives config round-trips instead of being inlined into `openclaw.json`. The substitution and preservation paths share the same token helpers to avoid grammar drift.

## User Impact

Operators can inject array/object config through environment variables by opting in with `${VAR:json}`. For example, with `ALLOW_FROM='["alice","bob"]'`, a config value of `"${ALLOW_FROM:json}"` resolves to the array `["alice","bob"]`. Plain `${VAR}` behavior, escaping (`$${VAR}`), and missing-var handling are unchanged, and authored `${VAR:json}` references are preserved across config writes. No migration is required.

## Evidence

Live terminal output from running the config loader (`resolveConfigEnvVars`) and the write-back path (`restoreEnvVarRefs`) from source via `node`, against a config file with `allowFrom: "${ALLOW_FROM:json}"` (opt-in) and a plain string-only `env.vars.TAGS: "${TAGS}"`, with `ALLOW_FROM='["alice","bob"]'` and `TAGS='["x","y"]'`. Console output:

```
[1] resolved config:
{
  "channels": { "discord": { "allowFrom": [ "alice", "bob" ] } },
  "env": { "vars": { "TAGS": "[\"x\",\"y\"]" } }
}
opt-in allowFrom isArray: true
plain TAGS stays string: true
[2] write-back (what gets persisted to disk):
{
  "channels": { "discord": { "allowFrom": "${ALLOW_FROM:json}" } },
  "env": { "vars": { "TAGS": "${TAGS}" } }
}
allowFrom ref preserved: true
```

This demonstrates all three guarantees: (1) `${ALLOW_FROM:json}` coerces to a real array; (2) plain `${TAGS}` stays a string even though its value is JSON-looking (string fields are safe); (3) on write-back the authored `${ALLOW_FROM:json}` reference is restored rather than inlined.

Focused tests: `src/config/env-substitution.test.ts` and `src/config/env-preserve.test.ts` (92 passed total), covering opt-in coercion, plain-`${VAR}` passthrough, embedded/scalar/malformed/primitive passthrough, unknown-modifier literal passthrough, and `${VAR:json}` write-back round-trip (restore + caller-changed-value cases). `pnpm tsgo` is clean.

target.title: fix(backup): sweep stale temp artifacts before creating new backup
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where interrupted `backup create` runs can leave stale backup staging directories and full-size temporary archive files behind. A hard kill, OOM, host reboot, or process restart skips the in-process cleanup block, so these artifacts can accumulate and consume disk space across later backup runs.

Fixes #95582

## Why This Change Was Made

The backup startup path now sweeps only OpenClaw-owned stale backup artifacts older than one hour before creating a new archive. The sweep is scoped to the selected backup staging temp root for `openclaw-backup-*` directories and to the requested output path's sibling temp files matching `<output basename>.<uuid>.tmp`, avoiding broad deletion of unrelated shared-temp files.

Active backup runs now write owner markers for both artifact classes. Startup cleanup checks those owner markers and skips artifacts whose owner PID is still live, so long-running or concurrent backups are not removed by an age-only sweep.

## User Impact

Users and operators can rerun `backup create` after a killed or interrupted backup and have old OpenClaw backup temp artifacts cleaned automatically before the new backup starts. Recent artifacts and artifacts owned by a live backup process remain protected.

## Evidence

- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `pnpm exec vitest run -c test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/backup-create.test.ts -t "sweepStaleBackupArtifacts"` -> 7 passed, 38 skipped
- `git diff --check` -> passed
- Real backup proof with a temporary HOME/state/config and seeded stale artifacts:

```text
beforeStagingExists=True
beforeArchiveTempExists=True
Backup archive: <temp>\backups\proof-backup.tar.gz
Included 2 paths:
- state: <temp>\state
- config: <temp>\openclaw.json
Created <temp>\backups\proof-backup.tar.gz
afterStagingExists=False
afterArchiveTempExists=False
backupExists=True
backupBytes=19407
```

- Earlier broader Windows validation note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/backup-create.test.ts` reached 28 passed before existing `createBackupArchive` tests failed on Windows with `EBUSY` unlinking temp SQLite files during test cleanup.
- Earlier broader Windows validation note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/backup.atomic.test.ts src/commands/backup.test.ts` had `backup.atomic.test.ts` pass; `backup.test.ts` had 13 passed and 1 Windows `EBUSY` cleanup failure on a temp SQLite WAL file.

target.title: fix(memory): skip placeholder short-term promotions
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Closes #80582.

Short-term memory promotion could treat markdown and QMD scaffolding as durable content. That included empty list/checklist/fence/table fragments, placeholder daily-note headings, generic daily/dreaming headings, QMD hunk wrappers, collapsed persisted heading prefixes, and mixed snippets that carried structural text alongside real content.

## Why This Change Was Made

The fix centralizes short-term snippet promotability in the promotion resolver and applies that resolver across recall recording, store normalization, grounded candidates, ranking, relocation/rehydration, and direct promotion application.

Placeholder-only snippets are skipped. Mixed snippets keep real content while dropping structural wrappers such as QMD hunk headers, generic daily/dreaming headings, collapsed heading prefixes, and bullet-prefixed collapsed heading prefixes. Ordinary non-generic bullets remain intact.

The diagnostic skipped recall event now has an additive `unpromotable-short-term-snippet` reason, with the Plugin SDK API baseline hash regenerated for the exported event type change.

## User Impact

Memory promotion should carry actual remembered content instead of daily-note scaffolding or QMD placeholder text. Existing meaningful snippets still promote, and legacy event readers continue to read recorded recall events while diagnostic readers can see skipped recall records.

## Evidence

Duplicate check:

- `gh pr list --repo openclaw/openclaw --state open --search "80582 in:body" --json number,title,url,updatedAt,isDraft,author`
- Result: no open PRs found.

Real memory-workspace proof:

- Command shape: `node --import tsx -e '<temp workspace proof script>'`
- Imported `extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-state.ts` and `extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.ts` directly.
- Used a temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and temp workspace.
- Created `memory/daily/2026-04-19.md` containing `## Tagesnotizen`, `-`, and `Rotate backups before deploy.`
- Recorded two memory hits from that file: placeholder snippet `@@ -1,2\n## Tagesnotizen\n-` and meaningful snippet `Rotate backups before deploy.`
- Ranked and applied with one-recall thresholds so the proof completes in one isolated run.

target.title: fix: preserve steered audio for inbound TTS
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When `tts.auto` is set to `"inbound"`, OpenClaw should use voice output for replies that answer inbound audio. A text-started reply run could later accept steered inbound audio while the active run was still streaming, but the final TTS decision only looked at the original text message. That meant the agent could answer the later audio while the final delivery still stayed text-only.

This is the same user-visible failure reported in #94049, but the fix avoids making steered audio a persisted session-state field.

## Why This Change Was Made

The inbound-audio fact belongs to the current reply operation, not durable session state. This change threads `currentInboundAudio` through the active embedded-run queue options, latches it on the in-memory `ReplyOperation` only after the active run accepts the steered message, and lets final TTS resolve `inboundAudio` from either the original message or that operation-local marker.

That keeps the behavior generic across channels and avoids WhatsApp-specific delivery logic, session-entry compatibility concerns, or changes to `packages/speech-core`.

## User Impact

Users with `tts.auto: "inbound"` now get voice output when an active reply run is steered by a later inbound audio message and the final answer is generated from that same operation.

Text-only replies and non-final TTS behavior continue to use the existing original-message classification unless steered audio was actually accepted into the active operation.

## Evidence

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/reply-run-registry.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.media-paths.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/runs.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- targeted `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on touched files
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

target.title: fix(memory-core): skip forced sync for healthy zero-hit search
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

A valid memory search can return zero hits while the index is marked dirty even though the index is otherwise healthy. Before this fix, the tool wrapper treated that dirty-only zero-hit as a reason to call `manager.sync({ reason: "search", force: true })` and wait inside the tool call. On hosts where the forced sync does not complete quickly, `memory_search` can hang until the tool timeout even though the correct user-facing answer is simply an empty result set.

This patch keeps the recall-preserving retry for non-QMD dirty indexes while preventing the QMD healthy dirty-only zero-hit path from forcing a blocking sync. Missing, invalid, empty, or unavailable index states still force sync.

## Summary

- skip blocking forced sync for healthy QMD zero-hit search results, including dirty-only QMD status
- preserve dirty zero-hit forced-sync retry for non-QMD or unknown backends
- keep forced sync for missing, invalid, empty, vector-unavailable, or FTS-unavailable memory states
- treat disabled vector/FTS modes as healthy instead of unavailable
- add regression coverage for QMD dirty healthy zero-hit no-sync and non-QMD dirty zero-hit retry behavior

## Evidence

Focused local verification on branch head `925495a4`:

```text
git diff --check
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/lestat/openclaw-fresh/workspace/external/openclaw-source-preservation

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  19 passed (19)
   Start at  20:29:09
   Duration  4.21s (transform 2.03s, setup 283ms, import 3.64s, tests 101ms, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 19.33s
```

Regression coverage now includes:

- `shouldForceSyncAfterZeroResults` returns `healthy_zero_hit` for QMD dirty healthy zero-hit status
- `shouldForceSyncAfterZeroResults` returns `dirty_index` for builtin or unknown dirty healthy zero-hit status
- `returns a healthy zero-hit without forcing sync when the index is ready` asserts one search call and zero sync calls
- `preserves the dirty zero-hit retry for non-qmd backends` asserts the wrapper syncs once, searches twice, and returns the second-search result
- disabled vector/FTS modes with `available: false` do not make the index unhealthy

Live installed-runtime proof before source promotion:

- `openclaw memory status --json` reported indexed and ready memory with `files: 159`, `chunks: 2519`, `dirty: false`, enabled/available FTS and vector, and `custom.indexIdentity.status: valid`
- a synthetic valid no-hit returned `0` results in about `491.6ms` with `forcedSync.attempted=false` and `forcedSync.reason=healthy_zero_hit` after gateway reload

## Safety Boundaries

- no model switch
- no production reindex
- no vector deletion
- no memory config change
- no secret/token output
- no unrelated Pi-hole/plugin work

target.title: fix(agent-runner): classify transient provider errors as fallback-worthy
target.body:
## Summary

When a primary provider returns an `upstream_error` / `HTTP 500` / transient server error, the embedded agent run result classifier discards it (returns null), so `runWithModelFallback` never advances to the next configured fallback model. The turn ends immediately with `LLM request failed` even when healthy fallback models are configured and ready.

Fixes #95519

## Changes

- **`result-fallback-classifier.ts`**: Renamed `classifyBusinessDenialErrorPayloadReason` → `classifyProviderErrorPayloadReason` and extended the switch statement to also pass through `server_error` and `timeout` failover reasons (previously only `auth`/`auth_permanent`/`billing` were passed through).
- **`result-fallback-classifier.test.ts`**: Updated the "does not retry non-business transport error payloads" test case to expect a proper fallback classification for `HTTP 500: internal server error` instead of null.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Provider transient errors (`upstream_error`, `HTTP 500`, server timeout) now correctly trigger configured model fallbacks instead of ending the turn immediately.

**Evidence type**: terminal

**Real setup tested**: Node v24.13.1, Linux x86_64, worktree branch on openclaw/openclaw@main

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/outcome-fallback-runtime-contract.test.ts
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
$ CI=true pnpm test -- src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  11 passed (11)

$ CI=true pnpm test -- src/agents/outcome-fallback-runtime-contract.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)

$ CI=true pnpm test -- src/agents/model-fallback.test.ts

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  82 passed (82)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: All 103 tests pass across 3 related test suites. The classifier now correctly identifies `server_error` and `timeout` provider errors as fallback-worthy alongside the existing `auth`/`auth_permanent`/`billing` classifications.

**What was not tested**: Live end-to-end verification with a real provider returning `upstream_error` was not performed. The fix is validated at the unit-test layer which directly covers the classification code path.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| result-fallback-classifier | ✅ | 11/11 passed |
| outcome-fallback-runtime-contract | ✅ | 10/10 passed |
| model-fallback | ✅ | 82/82 passed |

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**Yes** — users with configured fallback models will now see automatic fallback on transient provider errors (`upstream_error`, `HTTP 500`, timeouts) instead of immediate turn failure. This is the intended fix.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No**

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes` / `No`)

**No** — only the internal fallback classification path is changed; auth/auth_permanent errors already triggered fallback before this change.

What is the highest-risk area?
- Over-fallbacking if the classifier is too broad

How is that risk mitigated?
- Only `server_error` and `timeout` (two specific transient errors already identified by the existing `classifyFailoverReason` function) are added. All other classifications (format, rate_limit, overloaded, model_not_found, etc.) remain filtered out as before. The non-GPT model fallthrough path is unchanged.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

commands.author: SunnyShu0925

target.title: fix(approval): distinguish policy vs non-persistable reason when Allow Always unavailable
target.body:
Fixes #97069.

## What Problem This Solves

When a command includes shell redirection (e.g. `openclaw --version 2>&1`), Allow Always is correctly hidden because one-shot commands can't be safely persisted. But all nine approval prompt renderers (reply, forwarder, agent-facing, Control UI, reaction, embedded handler, Telegram handler, Telegram forwarding, and gateway API) incorrectly blame the effective approval policy—even when the effective policy is `ask=on-miss`. Users waste time misdiagnosing their approval policy.

## Why This Change Was Made

The root cause is that `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` returns the same `["allow-once", "deny"]` for both `ask=always` (policy) and one-shot persistence (non-persistable command). Downstream renderers can't tell the difference.

Rather than introducing a new typed protocol field (`allowAlwaysUnavailableReason`) — which #97081 attempted across 65 files — we thread the existing `ask` field (already present in `ExecApprovalRequestPayload`, `ExecApprovalPendingReplyParams`, `PendingApprovalView`, Control UI request state, and the gateway/node host pipeline) through the approval-pending data pipeline so all renderers can branch on it.

The fix is at the renderer layer, not the policy decision layer. Approval policy logic is unchanged.

## Root Cause

`src/infra/exec-approvals.ts:1824` — `resolveExecApprovalAllowedDecisions()` collapses both reasons (ask=always policy and one-shot persistence) into a single return value. Nine renderers check `!allowedDecisions.includes("allow-always")` and unconditionally emit the policy message.

Introduced by: gumadeiras `ba735d015809` (generic unavailable copy)
Made visible by: jesse-merhi `c9707ab635b9` (one-shot persistence for shell-redirected commands)
Confidence: clear

## User Impact

Users with `ask=on-miss` (or any non-always policy) who run commands with shell redirection will now see:

> Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

Instead of the misleading:

> The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

Users with `ask=always` see the same policy message as before.

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Approval prompt text blames policy when missing Allow Always is actually caused by one-shot/non-persistable command safety.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/exec-approval-prompt-text-97069`, base commit `origin/main`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

**Reply renderer** (the function that builds gateway-to-channel approval prompts):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload } from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts';

console.log('=== ask=always ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't1', approvalSlug: 's1',
  ask: 'always', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);

console.log('=== ask=on-miss ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload({
  approvalId: 't2', approvalSlug: 's2',
  ask: 'on-miss', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'],
  command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', host: 'gateway',
}).text);
"
```

**Forwarder renderer** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects through the channel-forwarding path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "
import { buildExecApprovalRequestMessage } from './src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts';

var now = Date.now();
var req = {
  id: 'exec-req-1', createdAtMs: now, expiresAtMs: now + 300000,
  request: { command: 'openclaw --version 2>&1', cwd: '/home/user/project',
    host: 'gateway', agentId: 'main', security: 'allowlist' },
};

console.log('=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'always' } }
));

console.log('=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===');
console.log(buildExecApprovalRequestMessage(
  { ...req, request: { ...req.request, ask: 'on-miss', unavailableDecisions: ['allow-always'] } }
));
"
```

**Evidence after fix** — real production renderer output:

**Reply renderer**:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.

=== ask=on-miss (one-shot) ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Forwarder** (real `ExecApprovalRequest` objects — same shape as the gateway production path):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "..."

=== ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because the effective policy requires approval every time.

=== ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
🔒 Exec approval required
...
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).
```

**Telegram approval paths** (same renderer called by `buildTelegramExecApprovalPendingPayload` and `approval-handler.runtime.ts`):

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "import ... from './src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts'; ..."

=== Telegram: ask=on-miss + allow-always excluded ===
Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted (e.g., shell redirection or dynamic content).

=== Telegram: ask=always + allow-always excluded ===
The effective approval policy requires approval every time, so Allow Always is unavailable.
```

| `ask` input | Message shown |
|---|---|
| `"always"` | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (unchanged) |
| `"on-miss"` (one-shot) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| `"off"` (full-auto with shell redirect) | "Allow Always is unavailable because this command cannot be persisted…" (fixed) |
| absent / null (plugin, legacy) | "The effective approval policy requires approval every time…" (safe default) |

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts \
    src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
    ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
# all passed
```

**Observed result after fix**: All nine approval prompt renderers now show the appropriate message. Policy → policy text, one-shot → non-persistable text, absent ask → policy (safe default). Production pipeline (gateway + node hosts → ExecToolDetails → embedded handler) threads `ask` through. The node path uses `approvalDecisionAsk` (`maxAsk(hostAsk, nodeAsk)`) — keeping the reasoning consistent with `allowedDecisions` computation. Telegram native and forwarding paths pass `ask` through `buildExecApprovalPendingReplyPayload`. Gateway API error path uses the same `isPolicyAlways` heuristic.

Additional CI guard checks pass locally: `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke` (dts emit), `pnpm ui:i18n:check` (all locales clean).

**What was not tested**: Live Gateway with Telegram/Discord channel approval delivery. Telegram terminal proof above exercises the exact same renderer functions called by the Telegram plugin's `buildTelegramExecApprovalPendingPayload` and `approval-handler.runtime.ts`. Mantis automated Telegram Desktop proof will further validate on push via `pull_request_target`.

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs \
    src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts \
    src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts \
    src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts \
    src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.test.ts \
    src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts \
    src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts \
    extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts \
    ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts
# all pass
```

New test cases added:
- `exec-approval-reply.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason when allow-always is excluded for non-policy reasons"
- `exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason when allow-always excluded for non-policy reasons"
- `approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts`: "shows non-persistable reason in reaction prompts when allow-always excluded for one-shot"

## Changes

- `src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts`: +5/−1 — branch on `params.ask` in reply renderer
- `src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts`: +5/−1 — branch on `request.request.ask` in forwarder
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts`: +6/−1 — accept `ask` in agent-facing pending message
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-types.ts`: +1 — add `ask` to `ExecToolDetails` approval-pending
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.ts`: +3 — thread `ask` through `buildExecApprovalPendingToolResult`
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts`: +1 — pass `hostAsk` to pending tool result
- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.ts`: +2 — pass `approvalDecisionAsk` to pending result and request registration (not raw `hostAsk` — matches the `maxAsk(hostAsk, nodeAsk)` value used to compute `allowedDecisions`)
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts`: +3 — read and forward `ask` from details
- `src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.ts`: +7/−1 — branch on `ask` in reaction manual instruction, with `approvalKind === "exec"` discriminator guard for `PendingApprovalView` union type
- `extensions/telegram/src/exec-approval-forwarding.ts`: +1 — pass `params.request.request.ask` to reply payload
- `extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.ts`: +1 — pass `params.view.ask` (exec only) to reply payload
- `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approval.ts`: +10/−7 — distinguish policy vs non-persistable in validateDecision error message
- `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.ts`: +11/−3 — use `allowAlwaysNonPersistable` i18n key for non-policy
- `ui/src/i18n/locales/en.ts`: +2 — new `allowAlwaysNonPersistable` key
- `ui/src/i18n/`: sync-generated fallback keys for all other locales
- 3 test files: +54 new tests covering one-shot render path

target.title: fix(plugin-sdk): update ssrf-runtime import path in cdp-proxy-bypass
target.body: ## What Problem This SolvesThe browser plugin fails to lazy-load in v2026.6.9 with a Package subpath not defined error. The ollama embedding provider had the same latent bug.**Root cause**: `ssrf-runtime-internal.ts` exports two functions (`registerManagedProxyBrowserCdpBypass`, `fetchConfiguredLocalOriginWithSsrFGuard`) but the subpath is excluded from `package.json` exports.**Fix**: Promote the two functions to the public `ssrf-runtime` export surface (+2 exports in `ssrf-runtime.ts`), update all consumer imports, and merge test mocks.## Evidence### Changes (5 files, +6/-10)| File | Change ||------|--------|| `src/plugin-sdk/ssrf-runtime.ts` | +2 exports || `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp-proxy-bypass.ts` | import to ssrf-runtime || `extensions/browser/src/browser/cdp-proxy-bypass.test.ts` | mock path to ssrf-runtime || `extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.ts` | import to ssrf-runtime || `extensions/ollama/src/embedding-provider.test.ts` | merged mocks || `extensions/browser/.../cdp.helpers.internal.test.ts` | merged mock || `extensions/browser/.../chrome.internal.test.ts` | mock path to ssrf-runtime |### After-fix: public ssrf-runtime now exports both functions### Private subpath infrastructure unchanged## Test plan- [x] 5 files changed (+6/-10 lines)- [x] All consumer imports point to public ssrf-runtime path- [x] All test mock paths match consumer imports- [x] No production code imports from ssrf-runtime-internal remain- [x] SDK infrastructure unchanged- [ ] Verify built/package browser lazy-load path- [ ] Verify ollama embedding provider lazy-loads correctlyFixes #96639Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: fix: recover terminal session status on visible inbound turns
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86827 — group chat sessions stuck in `failed` status silently drop all subsequent messages.

When a group chat session enters a terminal status (`failed`, `timeout`, or `killed`), the reply dispatch path returns a successful no-op (`queuedFinal: false`, zero dispatch counts) without starting an AI turn or delivering any user-visible error. All subsequent messages are silently discarded until a manual `/new` reset.

## Root Cause

Three independent mechanisms conspire to create the silent drop:

1. **Session state persistence**: `initSessionState()` reuses the existing session entry with its terminal `status` field intact, passing it downstream unchanged.
2. **Reply operation locking**: A stale `ReplyOperation` from the failed run remains registered in `replyRunRegistry`, causing `admitReplyTurn()` to return `skipped` with reason `active-run`.
3. **No fallback or diagnostic**: The `skipped` admission produces a valid dispatch result with zero counts, and no warning is logged — the message simply vanishes.

## Changes

### 1. Auto-recover terminal sessions (`session.ts`)

When a **visible** (non-cron/heartbeat) inbound message hits a session with recoverable terminal status, automatically clear the stale lifecycle fields (`status`, `startedAt`, `endedAt`, `runtimeMs`, `abortedLastRun`) while **preserving the `sessionId` and transcript continuity** — no session rotation.

### 2. Clear stale reply operations with race guard (`dispatch-from-config.ts`)

When `admitReplyTurn()` returns `skipped` with reason `active-run` but the persisted session is in terminal status, force-clear the stale reply operation via `forceClearReplyRunBySessionId()` and retry admission **once**. This breaks the deadlock without risking infinite retry loops.

**Concurrent recovery guard**: After a recovery turn clears the proven stale leftover and admits its own fresh operation, it is marked with a `terminalRecovery` flag. If a second visible message arrives against the same terminal snapshot before the first recovery clears the store state, the force-clear branch now skips operations marked as in-flight terminal recoveries. This prevents a sibling recovery turn from killing a fresh recovery that is already in flight, letting concurrent turns fall through to normal busy/wait handling instead.

### 3. Agent API path (`agent.ts`)

Mirror the same terminal recovery logic in the agent API dispatch path so agent API requests also clear terminal state for visible requests.

### 4. Diagnostics (`kernel.ts`, `types.ts`)

Add a structured warning log when a visible channel turn dispatch completes with `queuedFinal: false` and zero tool/block/final counts — a sentinel for future silent message drops. Excludes system turns (heartbeat/cron/exec) and observe-only admissions.

## Real behavior proof

Captured by driving the **real** runtime modules (`dispatchReplyFromConfig`, `initSessionState`, `reply-run-registry`) with verbose logging on, **no vitest and no module mocks** — only the production parameters the Gateway supplies plus a real on-disk JSON session store. A before/after contrast was produced by running the identical driver in a pre-fix worktree.

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A group-chat session left in a terminal lifecycle status (`failed`/`timeout`/`killed`) silently drops every later message — the stale lifecycle entry plus a leftover "active" reply operation block turn admission, so a subsequent visible inbound message gets no AI turn and no reply (#86827).
- **Real environment tested**: Compiled branch source at HEAD `ac52eb0b24`; macOS 15.3.1 (Darwin 24.3.0, arm64), Node `v24.10.0`. Product built with `pnpm build:docker` (exit 0); the recovery log string is present in compiled `dist/`. Evidence captured via `tsx` driving the real in-process dispatch/session/registry path. Before/after contrast run at pre-fix commit `8de37e1ce4` (parent of the first PR commit).
- **Failing proof before fix** (`8de37e1ce4`, identical driver + input): real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible group turn on a `status="failed"` session with a stale running reply op —
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  [dispatch] dispatch TIMED-OUT (no reply within 8s): visible turn stuck behind the stale terminal reply op -> SILENT DROP
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = null
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = true
  ```
  And real `initSessionState()` against an on-disk store: `RESULT: STILL-TERMINAL — session entry remains status=failed after a visible inbound turn`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm build:docker
  export OPENCLAW_HOME=$(mktemp -d) OPENCLAW_DISABLE_BUNDLED_PLUGINS=1 \
         OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_PROVIDERS=1 OPENCLAW_SKIP_CRON=1
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts   # drives real dispatchReplyFromConfig()
  node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_proof_driver.mts      # drives real initSessionState() + registry
  ```
  The driver seeds a real session store with a terminal `failed` group entry, registers a real stale running reply operation, then calls the real `dispatchReplyFromConfig()` for a visible inbound group message.
- **Evidence after fix (`ac52eb0b24`)**: copied live console output from running the real in-process driver `node_modules/.bin/tsx ./_dispatch_driver.mts` (drives the production `dispatchReplyFromConfig()`); redacted runtime log:
  ```
  [dispatch] registered stale running reply op; isActive=true
  [dispatch] calling REAL dispatchReplyFromConfig() for visible group turn on terminal session ...
  dispatch-from-config: cleared stale active reply operation for terminal session agent:main:telegram:group:<redacted>
  [dispatch] dispatch returned: {"queuedFinal":true,"counts":{"tool":0,"block":0,"final":1}}
  [dispatch] stale op result after dispatch = {"kind":"failed","code":"run_failed"}
  [dispatch] registry isActive after dispatch = false
  ```
  Real session lifecycle: `initSessionState -> isNewSession=false sessionId=<same> abortedLastRun=false`, and `AFTER turn -> status=undefined ... RESULT: RECOVERED — terminal lifecycle cleared, same sessionId reused (transcript preserved)`.
- **Observed result after fix**: A visible inbound message to a terminal session (1) emits the new recovery log, (2) force-fails the stale leftover op and frees the registry (`isActive=false`), (3) produces a real queued reply (`queuedFinal:true, counts.final:1`) instead of the pre-fix zero-count silent drop / indefinite block, and (4) clears the persisted terminal lifecycle fields while reusing the same `sessionId` (transcript preserved). The pre-fix checkout, given identical input, leaves the session `failed` and the turn stuck/dropped.
- **What was not tested**: The full Docker image build failed at the `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` layer due to transient npm-registry CDN errors fetching optional native packages (network, not OOM and not code), so no end-to-end live channel socket round-trip was performed; the proof drives the real in-process dispatch/session/registry runtime that a channel turn funnels into. The `agent.ts` Gateway RPC path and the `kernel.ts` zero-count warning were source-verified but not separately runtime-exercised here. Concurrency races (sibling visible-turn recovery, heartbeat non-clobber) are covered by the added unit tests. Architecture: arm64 (macOS) only; CI truth is Linux x64 Node 24.

## Tests

- **session.test.ts**: Verify failed group sessions recover without rotating the transcript
- **dispatch-from-config.test.ts**: Verify stale active reply operations are cleared for terminal sessions and admission is retried; verify two concurrent visible turns against the same terminal snapshot do not kill each other's recovery operations
- **agent.test.ts**: Verify terminal failed agent API sessions recover without session rotation; updated existing failed-session tests to verify terminal state clearing
- **kernel.test.ts**: Verify zero-count visible dispatch warning is emitted
- **agent-runner-execution.test.ts / agent-runner-memory.test.ts**: Updated mock `ReplyOperation` stubs with new `terminalRecovery` field and `markTerminalRecovery` method

All focused tests pass across 2 Vitest shards (512 cases).
